entries.author: Phyllis Cha

entries.title: Pete Crow-Armstrong, Cubs close to agreement on long-term contract extension: source
entries.summary: <p>MESA, Ariz. — Pete Crow-Armstrong celebrates his 24th birthday Wednesday. The icing on his cake will be knowing he’s on the verge of signing a long-term contract extension that will make him a Cub into his 30s.</p><p>A Gold Glove center fielder with elite speed who hit 31 homers and stole 35 bases for the Cubs in 2025, Crow-Armstrong is close to an agreement with the team that will be announced Thursday at Wrigley Field, a source said.</p><p>The Cubs would buy out Crow-Armstrong’s arbitration years and get cost certainty with the deal while giving financial security to the outfielder, who was scheduled to make $890,000 this season. Crow-Armstrong would have been eligible for arbitration in 2027 and free agency in 2031. The Cubs and Crow-Armstrong’s representatives were known to be discussing an extension on and off for about a year.</p><p>Terms of what Crow-Armstrong’s deal is expected to be were not immediately known. USA Today reported in excess of $100 million for at least six years. The Padres signed 22-year-old center fielder Jackson Merrill, similar to Crow-Armstrong in service time, to a nine-year, $135 million deal.</p><p>With face-of-the-franchise swagger and energy last season, Crow-Armstrong added 37 doubles and four triples to his home run total and became the fastest player to reach 30 homers and 30 stolen bases in a season. He was the first 30/30 Cub since Sammy Sosa in 1995. But while establishing himself as the best defensive center fielder in baseball, he faded offensively, producing a .634 OPS following the All-Star break after posting an .847 OPS that earned him an All-Star Game appearance and early MVP consideration.</p><p>A free swinger with a propensity to chase, Crow-Armstrong struck out 155 times while drawing just 29 walks. But in the end, he produced a 6.0 WAR, per Baseball Reference, second on the Cubs to Nico Hoerner (6.2). He led all outfielders with 24 Outs Above Average and finished ninth in MVP voting.</p><p>Crow-Armstrong was 3-for-10 in the Wild Card Series against the Padres and 2-for-19 in the NLDS against the Brewers with no extra base hits and one stolen base in the postseason. He was 2-for-18 in seven games in spring training, which concludes Tuesday, and hit two homers in one game for Team USA in the World Baseball Classic.</p><p>With designs on a deep postseason run, the Cubs <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/01/10/cubs-alex-bregman-agree-to-5-year-175-million-deal" >signed free agent Alex Bregman to a $175 million deal</a> in the offseason and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/01/07/chicago-cubs-in-serious-talks-with-miamimarlins-to-acquire-rhp-edward-cabrera-mlb-trade" >traded for Marlins right-hander Edward Cabrera</a>. Their projected luxury tax payroll for 2025, per Spotrac, was $224,462,380 at Crow-Armstrong’s current salary, ranking them 11th among major league teams.</p><p>The 19th overall draft pick by the Mets out of high school in the 2020 draft, Crow-Armstrong was acquired by the Cubs in a trade for Javier Baez and Trevor Williams a year later. The outfielder broke in with the Cubs in 2023, playing 13 games, and in his first full season in 2024 batted .237/.286/.384 with 10 home runs and 27 stolen bases in 123 games.<br></p>
entries.author: Daryl Van Schouwen

entries.title: Bulls executive VP Arturas Karnisovas running out of people to blame
entries.summary: <p>At some point very soon there’s not going to be anyone else to blame.</p><p>No medical trainer, no analytics expert, no shooting coach, not even the equipment guy that cleans the jerseys.</p><p>That’s when it will get very interesting for Bulls executive vice president of basketball operations Arturas Karnisovas. Because even if it’s accidental, he’ll have to stumble across a mirror.</p><p>Karnisovas’ creation continues toiling in NBA no man’s land: Too untalented to even be in the annual “Bulls Play-In Invitational,” too naïve to understand they shouldn’t be playing veteran free-agent-to-be Collin Sexton long enough to score 25 points in Monday’s win over Houston.</p><p>The suit finally chose the proper path of tanking – albeit at least a season too late – and still can’t carry out the assignment correctly.</p><p>So where does the finger point this offseason from his perch?</p><p>Coach Billy Donovan might not be an option. There’s been growing momentum around the building that Donovan could step away from the mess and take a season to re-evaluate what he wants from the profession. It would make sense considering how emotional the year has been for the Hall of Famer, losing both his father and mother-in-law within weeks of each other.</p><p>Plus, while Donovan is the ultimate optimist and isn’t close to being done coaching, it is now six seasons without a proven elite talent, instead being asked to build a brick house out of straw and mud.</p><p>Could general manager Marc Eversley be the one left holding the bag in Karnisovas’ blame game? That’s not a stretch, either. While the Sun-Times reported last April that all final decisions not only run through Karnisovas, but anyone that questions those decisions is quickly escorted from the building, there have been whispers that Eversley hasn’t been 100% on the same page with his boss the last few years.</p><p>Eversley’s problem, however, is even if he tries to go elsewhere on his own, what's on the resume he hands out?</p><p>“Go getter who is a team player and proactive in building six seasons of failure.”</p><p>That doesn’t exactly scream great candidate.</p><p>No, Eversley’s only way out is organizational success or his stuff left in a cardboard box at the Advocate Center loading dock. The latter seems more realistic at this point.</p><p>Maybe the roster takes another cleansing. Unlikely considering the strip job that went down at the February trade deadline. What’s left to go? Karnisovas has already said that the roster centers around Matas Buzelis and Josh Giddey.</p><p>Rookie Noa Essengue’s name was also thrown out there by Karnisovas, but if the fortunes of the franchise fall on a 19-year-old French kid that weighs 190 pounds and has already physically been overwhelmed with a season-ending injury, yeah, that shouldn’t comfort anyone.</p><p>But never fear, the loaded 2026 draft class is here!</p><p>A collection of talent that has game changers one through four and possible game changers five through nine before there’s a dip. The problem is the Bucks and Bulls are battling for positioning at No. 9 and Milwaukee seems much better at it the last few weeks.</p><p>It feels like the Bulls will finish in the No. 10 spot and then need lottery luck to bounce their way.</p><p>Bulls fans might want to start preparing for the 63.4% odds of picking No. 10 – if that’s where they end up – and embracing the addition of a guard like Labaron Philon or a big like Yaxel Lendeborg while other teams in the Eastern Conference are loading up on AJ Dybantsa, Darryn Peterson and Cameron Boozer.</p><p>That’s fine with Karnisovas because he’ll still have his “competitive integrity” intact.</p><p>What he won’t have? Many more people to blame for his failures and still not a mirror in sight.</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Anton Frondell to make Blackhawks debut next to Connor Bedard: 'He can handle it'
entries.summary: <p>ELMONT, N.Y. — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> rookie Anton Frondell stepped on the ice Tuesday morning at UBS Arena and, frankly, felt "very nervous."</p><p>Just three days earlier, his season in Sweden had <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/21/blackhawks-anton-frondell-nhl-debut-connor-bedard-linemate-rebuild-success" target="_blank" >ended</a> in a disappointing playoff loss, after which he almost immediately received a call from the Hawks: "You're coming."</p><p>The 18-year-old forward made it to Long Island on Monday in time for a dinner with his new teammates, starting the process of getting to know them. His parents and sister are also traveling with him for now.</p><p>He will make his much-anticipated NHL debut Tuesday against the Islanders and rookie phenom Matthew Schaefer, who was drafted two picks ahead of Frondell in the top three last summer. </p><p>"It felt hard to breathe out there [this morning], but now I'm ready for the game, hopefully," Frondell said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Anton Frondell is on the ice for Blackhawks morning skate, wearing #15: <a href="https://t.co/meMxZrFUhX">pic.twitter.com/meMxZrFUhX</a></p>— Ben Pope (@BenPopeCST) <a href="https://twitter.com/BenPopeCST/status/2036465335793037546?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 24, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Hawks coach Jeff Blashill immediately slotted Frondell on the first line next to Connor Bedard and Ryan Greene, demoting Andre Burakovsky down to the third line in an unexpected move that delighted fans.</p><p>Frondell also slotted in on the top power-play unit, occupying the right flank — the spot where he unleashed some highlight-reel one-timers in a dominant showing for Sweden in the world junior championships in December.</p><p>"If I didn't think he could handle the spots I'm putting him in, I wouldn't do it right away, because you don't want to set somebody up for struggles," Blashill said. "I've watched him a lot. I know his game pretty well — with the exception of being with him day-to-day — and I think he can handle it. I think he's prepared at this moment to walk in and have success in those spots. </p><p>"Now, does that mean it's going to be easy sailing? No, this league is a hard league. There's going to be ups and downs throughout the course of his career, much less these next few weeks."</p><p>Blashill's message directly to Frondell was a bit simpler than that, though.</p><p>"You're going to make mistakes, and I don't care about mistakes," Blashill told him. "We care about learning from him, which we will do. But don't be afraid to make mistakes. Go out and play."</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/21/blackhawks-anton-frondell-nhl-debut-connor-bedard-linemate-rebuild-success" >Blackhawks about to get first glimpse of Anton Frondell, whose NHL success could determine fate of rebuild</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p><h3><b>Visa at last</b></h3><h3></h3><p>It's almost comical that Frondell will end up making his NHL debut before Sacha Boisvert, considering the latter finished his college season and joined the Hawks over a week ago now.</p><p>Boisvert's wait for a U.S. work visa proved excruciatingly long, but he finally received it Tuesday morning and was able to take part in morning skate. </p><p>Blashill said Boisvert will debut later on this four-game road trip. Thursday against the Flyers seems likely to be the day.</p><h3><b>Demoted at last</b></h3><p>After 26 games with just two points to his name, Burakovsky has finally been taken out of the top six, at least for now.</p><p>"He's done a ton of good stuff," Blashill said. "He's tracked extremely hard. He's stopped in the 'D'-zone. He's done all the things I've asked of him. His production hasn't been to the level that he expects or we want, [even though] the chances have been there. It's a chance for him to give us some depth scoring down the lineup a little bit, which we need."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Blackhawks lines in morning skate at Islanders:<br><br>Greene-Bedard-Frondell<br>Bertuzzi-Nazar-Lardis<br>Burakovsky-Donato-Mikheyev<br>Teravainen-Lafferty-Slaggert<br>Boisvert rotating in<br><br>Vlasic-Levshunov<br>Kaiser-Rinzel<br>Del Mastro-Crevier<br><br>Soderblom in starter’s crease</p>— Ben Pope (@BenPopeCST) <a href="https://twitter.com/BenPopeCST/status/2036466329490685983?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 24, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p></p><h3><b>Swedish lessons</b></h3><p>Frondell mentioned being proud of the fact he scored more goals this season (20) for Djurgardens, his Swedish club, in the country's top pro league than he did last season (11) in their second division — albeit in more games.</p><p>He most noticed the increase in physicality between the two leagues, however, and it took him time to adjust to that, even with his sturdy 6-1, 198-pound frame.</p><p>"Everyone was stronger, I felt like," Frondell said. "It was experienced players who know how to battle in the corners, know how to use their bodies. Sometimes I felt that. I got run over a couple times, and tried to learn from it. It's a hard game, hockey. You need to battle, and I like to do that."</p><p>His play really took off during the second half of the season after winning gold at the WJC, and he said the memory of that fueled him and fellow Djurgardens youngsters Victor Eklund (an Islanders prospect) and Viggo Bjorck (a projected lottery pick this year).</p><p>"All of us brought that gold energy, if you could say, back home," he added. "Whenever we had a tough day at Djurgardens, we could still think back to the gold-medal game and find some energy."</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Juez dictamina que el toque de queda frente a centro de Broadview viola la Primera Enmienda
entries.summary: <p>Un juez federal anuló el domingo el toque de queda del poblado de Broadview sobre las protestas, dictaminando que las restricciones impuestas por los líderes suburbanos han violado los derechos de los manifestantes bajo la Primera Enmienda.</p><p>El abogado de Chicago, Robert Held, solicitó una orden de restricción preliminar <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2025/11/13/lawyer-seeking-preliminary-injunction-in-lawsuit-against-broadview-over-protest-hours-appears-outside-ice-facility" target="_blank" >el otoño pasado</a> como parte de una demanda federal contra Broadview para anular la orden ejecutiva de la alcaldesa Katrina Thompson, que estableció límites de tiempo y áreas designadas para las protestas afuera de la instalación del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) en Broadview.</p><p>El juez de distrito Edmond Chang otorgó la orden de restricción preliminar, escribiendo en su decisión que las “restricciones actuales de Broadview no pueden mantenerse bajo la Primera Enmienda”.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-930000" name="module-930000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Held calificó la decisión como una “victoria conmovedora”.</p><p>“El juez analizó los hechos y la ley y reprimió a la alcaldesa”, dijo Held al Sun-Times. “Esta es una victoria no sólo para los manifestantes, sino para cualquier persona en este país que se preocupe por la Primera Enmienda y la Constitución”.</p><p>La propiedad de ICE se convirtió en el escenario <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/11/17/manifestantes-heridos-y-21-arrestados-tras-enfrentamientos-en-protesta-en-centro-de-broadview" target="_blank" >de protestas importantes</a> contra el “Operativo Midway Blitz”, la agresiva campaña de deportación de la administración de Trump en Chicago y sus suburbios el año pasado.</p><p>En respuesta, Thompson emitió varias órdenes ejecutivas restringiendo dónde y cuándo las personas pueden protestar en la instalación, que incluyeron el establecimiento de “zonas de libre expresión” y un toque de queda diario de 9 a.m. a 6 p.m. para las manifestaciones.</p><p>Held presentó la queja federal en octubre contra Thompson y el jefe de la Policía de Broadview, Thomas Mills, en un esfuerzo por anular las restricciones de protesta de la Municipalidad.</p><p>Al otorgar la orden de restricción preliminar, Chang dijo que los límites diarios en curso sobre las protestas “no logran sobrevivir al escrutinio de la Primera Enmienda”. Sin embargo, se les permite a los funcionarios mantener las zonas de protesta y establecer “límites más específicos para protestas específicas en días específicos”.</p><p>Un portavoz de Broadview no respondió de inmediato a una solicitud de comentarios.</p><p><i>Contribuyó: Violet Miller </i></p><p>https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/6d/90/bf9520534073a8b9fdcd01ee8bdc/broadviewpiopinion.pdf</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Kaitlin Washburn

entries.title: What to know about the killing of Sheridan Gorman
entries.summary: <p>The fatal shooting of Loyola student Sheridan Gorman has rocked the school’s community and attracted national attention.</p><p>Here’s what to know about the incident and where things currently stand in the alleged killer's criminal case.</p><h3>Who was Sheridan Gorman? </h3><p>Gorman was an 18-year-old freshman at Loyola University Chicago from Yorktown, New York. She was a member of the campus Christian organization Cru and was studying business.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/20/chicago-loyola-sheridan-gorman-rogers-park-crime-shooting-murder" target="_blank" >Friends and family describe her</a> as a kind and positive person who always had a smile on her face.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-230000" name="image-230000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d35eece/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc6%2F78%2Fa151dcc54f15b41dcb698ddc3a27%2Funtitled-design-53.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3bd775e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc6%2F78%2Fa151dcc54f15b41dcb698ddc3a27%2Funtitled-design-53.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d35eece/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc6%2F78%2Fa151dcc54f15b41dcb698ddc3a27%2Funtitled-design-53.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Sheridan Gorman</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“She was full of life, full of kindness, and full of a love that she gave freely to everyone around her,” Gorman’s parents wrote in a statement. “She made people feel seen. She made people feel valued. Whether it was her friends, her family, or someone she had just met, Sheridan had a way of leaving people better than she found them.”</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.gofundme.com/f/support-for-the-gorman-family-cz6ns" target="_blank" >A fundraiser is gathering donations</a> for Gorman’s memorial and a scholarship in her honor. Over $150,000 was raised as of Tuesday.</p><h3>What happened to her? </h3><p>Gorman and her friends took an early morning stroll around 1:30 a.m. on March 19 to the pier at Tobey Prinz Beach — just north of the Rogers Park campus. They were hoping to capture photos of the city skyline and potentially the Northern Lights, her parents said in a statement.</p><p>Gorman was the first to reach the end of the pier when she spotted a masked gunman. He fired a single shot as Gorman and her friends fled, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/23/chicago-crime-trump-sheridan-gorman-loyola-murder-shooting-deportation-immigration" target="_blank" >according to prosecutors</a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f10000" name="image-f10000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f83a857/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6794x3813+0+360/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F30%2F24%2F26133b284436886ccd6151722e2c%2Frogersparkhomicide-260320-04.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e94f323/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6794x3813+0+360/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F30%2F24%2F26133b284436886ccd6151722e2c%2Frogersparkhomicide-260320-04.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c72302e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6794x3813+0+360/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F30%2F24%2F26133b284436886ccd6151722e2c%2Frogersparkhomicide-260320-04.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Chicago Police Department officers investigate Thursday morning on the pier at Tobey Prinz Beach off West Pratt Boulevard in Rogers Park hours after 18-year-old Loyola University student Sheridan Gorman was shot to death." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c72302e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6794x3813+0+360/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F30%2F24%2F26133b284436886ccd6151722e2c%2Frogersparkhomicide-260320-04.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4ffeb49/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6794x3813+0+360/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F30%2F24%2F26133b284436886ccd6151722e2c%2Frogersparkhomicide-260320-04.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c72302e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6794x3813+0+360/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F30%2F24%2F26133b284436886ccd6151722e2c%2Frogersparkhomicide-260320-04.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago Police Department officers investigate Thursday morning on the pier at Tobey Prinz Beach off West Pratt Boulevard in Rogers Park hours after 18-year-old Loyola University student Sheridan Gorman was shot to death.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The friends ran for cover when the shot rang out. But then they saw Gorman on the ground and went to her aid. She had been shot in the back, prosecutors said — not in the head as police initially reported. She was pronounced dead at the scene, according to authorities.</p><p>One of Gorman’s friends who witnessed the shooting told the Sun-Times that “it didn’t seem like it was intentionally targeted toward any of us.”</p><p>“It seemed like he was there for a reason, and we were just [in the] wrong place, wrong time. I’m guessing, because there was not another shot after that. It was just one,” the friend said.</p><h3>Who is the suspect? </h3><p>Jose Medina, 25, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/22/ice-detainer-loyola-student-killing-sheridan-gorman" target="_blank" >was arrested and charged in Gorman’s murder</a>. He faces charges of first-degree murder, attempted murder and aggravated discharge of a firearm.</p><p>He has yet to make his first appearance in court because he is currently hospitalized with tuberculosis. A full detention hearing was postponed until Friday, but <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/23/chicago-crime-trump-sheridan-gorman-loyola-murder-shooting-deportation-immigration " target="_blank" >prosecutors provided a brief accounting</a> of the shooting in court on Monday.</p><p>Video captured Medina walking from the beach into a building in the 6800 block of North Sheridan Road, according to an arrest report. He wore all black clothing and a mask and had a “distinct gait.”</p><p>Police recovered black clothing and a .40 caliber handgun inside Medina’s apartment, prosecutors said. The gun matched the shell casing found at the shooting scene.</p><p>The arrest report shows police worked closely with U.S. Customs and Border Protection during the investigation. CBP used facial recognition to identify the man in the video as Medina, according to the report.</p><p>Records show Medina was born in Venezuela and at one point lived in a temporary migrant shelter at the Leone Beach Park Field House.</p><p>U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement issued an arrest detainer on March 22 naming Medina before police announced the charges. The Department of Homeland Security claims Medina entered the U.S. illegally. </p><p>Chicago police have not commented on his immigration status. Prosecutors also did not share that information in court Monday.</p><p>In May 2023, federal officials say he came into contact with Border Patrol agents and was released.</p><p>About a month later, he faced a single misdemeanor charge for shoplifting $132.50 of merchandise from the State Street Macy’s in June 2023. A judge issued a warrant for him in that case after he failed to appear for a court date. Records show the outstanding warrant is still active.</p><h3>How has Gorman’s death become politicized? </h3><p>President Donald Trump and his administration have used Gorman’s death to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/23/chicago-crime-trump-sheridan-gorman-loyola-murder-shooting-deportation-immigration" target="_blank" >justify his push for mass deportations</a>.</p><p>Acting Assistant DHS Secretary Lauren Bis claimed in a statement Gorman was “failed by open border policies and sanctuary politicians.”</p><p>This isn't the first time the Trump administration has used a young woman's death to make the case that undocumented immigrants put Americans in danger. However, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/23/chicago-crime-trump-sheridan-gorman-loyola-murder-shooting-deportation-immigration" target="_blank" >numerous studies have found</a> that immigrants without legal status are less likely to commit serious crimes than U.S.-born citizens.</p><p>The killing of Laken Riley, a nursing student in Georgia who was fatally attacked in 2024 by an immigrant, spurred federal legislation that mandates detention of undocumented immigrants accused of theft, burglary, assaulting a law enforcement officer and any crime that causes death or serious injury.</p><p>Operation Midway Blitz was dedicated to Katie Abraham, an Illinois native killed in 2025 by a drunken driver who allegedly lacked legal status. Notably, Abraham's mother <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagotribune.com/2025/10/21/opinion-katie-abraham-operation-midway-blitz/" target="_blank" >wrote an op-ed</a> last fall decrying the administration's use of her daughter's name in immigration enforcement operations.</p><p>“Sheridan Gorman’s life was allegedly taken from her at the hands of a criminal alien shielded by the anti-American sanctuary policies of the city of Chicago and the state of Illinois,” U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement said on social media Monday.</p><p>Legislation signed by former Illinois Gov. Bruce Rauner, a Republican, limits cooperation between local police and federal immigration authorities. The Illinois TRUST Act generally bars police from sharing information with immigration agents or handing over people in custody unless a federal criminal warrant is issued.</p><p>Gov. JB Pritzker issued a response saying, “Violent crime has no place in our streets.”</p><p>“We expect the alleged perpetrator to be held accountable to the fullest extent of the law,” Pritzker said in a statement. “The Trump Administration needs to stop politicizing heinous tragedies and instead focus on real solutions, like reinstating federal funds to prevent violence that support our public safety efforts.”</p><p>Panas in Chicago, a group that works with Venezuelan migrants, <a class="Link" href="https://www.facebook.com/panasenchicago" target="_blank" >said in a statement</a> the organization stands with Gorman’s family.</p><p>“Violence does not represent us,” the statement reads. “The Venezuelan community is made up of hardworking individuals with values, faith and a genuine commitment to contributing positively to the society that has welcomed us. We categorically reject any act of violence, regardless of who commits it. One individual’s actions, no matter how serious, do not define an entire community.”</p><h3><b>What’s next in the case? </b></h3><p>Medina, who remains hospitalized with tuberculosis, is scheduled to make his first full court appearance on Friday for a pretrial detention hearing. Judge Luciano Panici ordered he remain in custody pending the full hearing.</p><p>A community vigil and a private service will be held for Gorman on March 28 in Yorktown, New York.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times staff

entries.title: Broadview mayor demands closure of ICE facility, reimbursement of village, business losses
entries.summary: <p>The mayor of west suburban Broadview is calling on the federal government to shut down a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/01/broadview-immigration-processing-center-detention-ice-dhs" target="_blank" ><u>de facto immigration detention cente</u></a><u>r</u> that was the epicenter of protests — and violent responses from federal agents — that engulfed the small village last fall.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/immigration/2025/10/04/broadview-mayor-katrina-thompson-ice-detention-center-arrests-kristi-noem-gregory-bovino" target="_blank" ><u>Mayor Katrina Thompson</u></a> is also asking the feds for a more than $700,000 reimbursement to make up for operational costs incurred by the village and economic losses suffered by two businesses adjacent to the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement facility during Operation Midway Blitz, the intense deportation campaign that peaked from September through November.</p><p>She made the requests during a visit to Washington, D.C., earlier this month and in a letter to several members of the Illinois congressional delegation.</p><p>“Broadview did not ask for this operation, and our residents, our police officers, our firefighters, our business owners should not be the ones paying for it,” Thompson was quoted as saying in a news release. “I advocated for federal reimbursement for communities like ours impacted by ICE operations, emphasizing that local taxpayers should not bear the cost of federal responsibilities. I also pushed for the closure of the Broadview ICE Processing Center, centering dignity, safety, and a stronger vision for our community.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-640000" name="image-640000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4cbedd1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa0%2F83%2F88d99f8549f9afd1f1a0f334e1d3%2Fbroadviewtownhall-110425-15.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ca8c225/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa0%2F83%2F88d99f8549f9afd1f1a0f334e1d3%2Fbroadviewtownhall-110425-15.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3e67470/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa0%2F83%2F88d99f8549f9afd1f1a0f334e1d3%2Fbroadviewtownhall-110425-15.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Mayor Katrina Thompson addresses public commenters comments during a board meeting at the Broadview Village Hall in suburban Broadview, Monday, Nov. 3, 2025. | Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3e67470/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa0%2F83%2F88d99f8549f9afd1f1a0f334e1d3%2Fbroadviewtownhall-110425-15.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f4e1827/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa0%2F83%2F88d99f8549f9afd1f1a0f334e1d3%2Fbroadviewtownhall-110425-15.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3e67470/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa0%2F83%2F88d99f8549f9afd1f1a0f334e1d3%2Fbroadviewtownhall-110425-15.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Mayor Katrina Thompson is looking to push Broadview past the trouble surrounding the ICE facility in the western suburb.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The municipal costs associated with responding to activity around the ICE facility amounted to $361,536, according to Broadview officials.</p><p>Records obtained by the Chicago Sun-Times through a freedom of information request show $71,185 of that sum was paid as police overtime, with more than $3,000 for additional personal protective equipment for officers. The village spent about $41,000 on legal costs, including for a federal lawsuit to remove a fence erected around the ICE facility. Ambulance transfers from the ICE facility’s address cost another nearly $250,000. And overtime for fire and ambulance personnel cost about $12,100.</p><p>Even before the requested federal reimbursement, it was still unclear whether the village would otherwise recoup any of that money, such as EMT transports that might be paid through Medicaid insurance.</p><p>Broadview officials also noted $353,813 in losses for two local businesses within two blocks of the ICE facility: Reynolds Advanced Materials and Wagner Brass Foundry, Inc.</p><p>The turmoil that roiled the village of about 8,000 residents in the fall spilled onto the front lawns and backyards of many neighbors. Hundreds of people showed up for near daily protests outside the facility where the vast majority of immigrants arrested during Operation Midway Blitz were taken for processing before being moved to detention facilities in the Midwest. Conditions described by detainees and advocates as “inhumane” festered in the overcrowded building, where people slept on floors with limited access to food or water.</p><p>Demonstrators, including clergy, were met with force from federal agents with ICE and U.S. Customs and Border Protection who often chased protesters through neighborhoods and shot rubber bullets and tear gas into the crowds.</p><p>In her letter to lawmakers, Thompson asked for help to close the facility and transfer its ownership from the federal government to the village for redevelopment as a museum. The “Broadview Justice Corridor,” as she called the project, would turn the ICE facility into a “national center dedicated to immigration history, civil rights education, civic leadership, and cultural tourism.</p><p>“This transformation would convert a site associated with fear, protest, and division into one that promotes education, reconciliation, and national dialogue,” Thompson wrote in the letter, estimating the proposal would draw up to 250,000 visitors annually and generate up to $31 million in economic activity.</p><p>Thompson’s appeal to the two senators and six representatives, all Democrats, is unlikely to sway President Donald Trump’s hard-line administration, which has looked to open new immigration detention centers in the past year rather than close any ICE facilities. Some of the lawmakers who received Thompson’s appeal were criticized by administration officials and denied entry to the Broadview facility last year as they sought to inspect the conditions.</p>
entries.author: Nader Issa

entries.title: Trump condena el asesinato de estudiante de Loyola mientras promueve deportaciones
entries.summary: <p>A medida que el presidente Donald Trump utilizó el “devastador” asesinato de la estudiante de la Universidad Loyola de Chicago, Sheridan Gorman, para justificar su impulso hacia las deportaciones masivas, el presunto hombre armado permanecía hospitalizado y se perdió su audiencia judicial inicial en la tarde del lunes.</p><p>José G. Medina, un inmigrante venezolano, fue <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/23/migrante-venezolano-acusado-de-asesinato-de-la-estudiante-de-la-universidad-loyola" target="_blank" >acusado de asesinato en primer grado</a>, intento de asesinato, descarga agravada de un arma de fuego y posesión ilegal de un arma en relación con el ataque del jueves cerca de la playa Tobey Prinz, según los registros judiciales del Condado de Cook.</p><p>Trump les dijo a los periodistas el lunes que había sido informado sobre el asesinato de Gorman, argumentando que el tiroteo mortal mostró la necesidad de intensificar los esfuerzos de aplicación de la ley de inmigración.</p><p>“Esta persona entró por la política de puerta abierta de [el expresidente] Joe Biden y tenemos otros”, dijo Trump. “Los estamos sacando por decenas de miles. Estamos haciendo un gran trabajo, pero es una pena”.</p><p>Medina, de 25 años, era buscado con una orden de arresto en un caso local de robo en tiendas en el momento de su último arresto.</p><p>La familia de Gorman dijo que estaban “profundamente decepcionados por las políticas y fracasos que permitieron que este individuo permaneciera en una posición para cometer este crimen”.</p><p>“Cuando los sistemas fallan —ya sea por decisiones de liberación, falta de coordinación o falta de voluntad para actuar— las consecuencias no son abstractas. Son reales”, dijo la familia en un comunicado.</p><p>“Y en nuestro caso, son permanentes. Este caso debe ser presentado en la máxima medida de la ley estatal y federal. No puede haber vacíos, no puede haber atajos, y no puede haber segundas oportunidades que pongan a otros en riesgo. La responsabilidad debe ser completa”.</p><p>Una <a class="Link" href="https://www.gofundme.com/f/support-for-the-gorman-family-cz6ns" target="_blank" >recaudación de fondos en línea</a> que busca reunir dinero para un fondo memorial y de becas en honor a Gorman casi había alcanzado los $150,000 la tarde del lunes. La meta subió a $180,000.</p><p>Los fiscales proporcionaron pocos detalles adicionales sobre el caso criminal de Medina durante la breve audiencia del lunes.</p><p>Medina se perdió la corte porque actualmente está hospitalizado con tuberculosis, dijeron los funcionarios. El juez Luciano Panici ordenó que permaneciera "bajo custodia" a la espera de una audiencia completa el viernes.</p><p>Gorman, de 18 años, salió a caminar hacia la costa, justo al norte del campus de Loyola, temprano el jueves para tomar fotos del horizonte, dijeron los fiscales. Fue la primera en llegar al final del muelle cerca de la playa Tobey Prinz cuando vio al tirador enmascarado escondido cerca del faro.</p><p>Disparó un tiro mientras Gorman y sus amigos huían, dijeron los fiscales. Gorman fue alcanzada por un tiro en la espalda, no en la cabeza, como informó inicialmente la policía.</p><p>Una de las amigas de Gorman que presenció el tiroteo <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/20/chicago-loyola-sheridan-gorman-rogers-park-crime-shooting-murder" target="_blank" >dijo al Chicago Sun-Times</a> que "no parecía que estuviera dirigido intencionadamente hacia ninguno de nosotros".</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-3ee6bc" name="image-3ee6bc"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" to death, Thursday, March 19, 2026." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5068012/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6818x3826+0+606/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd6%2F30%2F872fd020461881bad76323c2496f%2Frogersparkhomicide-260320-02.jpg data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Los oficiales del Departamento de Policía de Chicago investigan en el muelle de la playa Tobey Prinz después de que la estudiante de 18 años de la Universidad Loyola, Sheridan Gorman, fuera asesinada a tiros el jueves.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Un video captó a Medina caminando desde la escena hacia un edificio en la cuadra 6800 al norte de Sheridan Road, según un informe de arresto. Llevaba ropa completamente negra y una máscara y tenía una “caminata distintiva”.</p><p>La policía recuperó ropa negra y una pistola calibre .40 dentro del apartamento de Medina, dijeron los fiscales. El arma coincidía con el casquillo encontrado en la escena del tiroteo.</p><p>El informe de arresto muestra que la policía trabajó en estrecha colaboración con la Oficina de Aduanas y Protección Fronteriza (CBP) durante la investigación. La CBP, que fue fundamental en la agresiva campaña de deportación en Chicago el año pasado, utilizó el reconocimiento facial para identificar al hombre en el video como Medina, según el informe.</p><p>CBP es parte del Departamento de Seguridad Nacional (DHS) que identificó públicamente a Medina como el sospechoso antes de que los fiscales del Condado de Cook aprobaran los cargos. El departamento también anunció que había emitido una orden de detención para Medina.</p><p>DHS dijo que Medina había sido detenido por la Patrulla Fronteriza en mayo de 2023, liberado y luego arrestado nuevamente dos meses después por robo en tiendas. Tenía una orden activa en el caso de robo, según los registros judiciales.</p><p>La administración de Trump ha utilizado las muertes de otras jóvenes para argumentar que la inmigración indocumentada pone en peligro a los estadounidenses. Sin embargo, los esfuerzos por enmarcar el crimen como la fuerza motriz detrás de su misión de deportación han sido subvertidos en ocasiones por datos e investigaciones.</p><p>El asesinato de Laken Riley, una estudiante de enfermería en Georgia que fue fatalmente atacada por un inmigrante, impulsó una legislación que exige la detención y fortalece la aplicación de la ley contra inmigrantes indocumentados acusados de ciertos delitos.</p><p>El Operativo Midway Blitz, la campaña de deportación desenfrenada que azotó el área de Chicago el otoño pasado, fue dedicada a Katie Abraham, originaria de Illinois asesinada por un conductor ebrio que supuestamente carecía de estatus legal.</p><p>“La vida de Sheridan Gorman fue supuestamente arrebatada por un extranjero criminal protegido por las políticas de santuario antiamericanas de la Ciudad de Chicago y del estado de Illinois”, dijo el Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) en las redes sociales el lunes.</p><p>El gobernador JB Pritzker emitió una respuesta el lunes, diciendo, "El crimen violento no tiene lugar en nuestras calles".</p><p>"Esperamos que el presunto perpetrador sea responsabilizado en la máxima medida de la ley", dijo Pritzker en un comunicado. "La administración de Trump necesita dejar de politizar tragedias atroces y en su lugar enfocarse en soluciones reales, como restablecer los fondos federales para prevenir la violencia que apoyen nuestros esfuerzos de seguridad pública".</p><p>Hablando después de la corte el lunes, el concejal Ray López (15º) instó a la Municipalidad y al estado a enmendar sus leyes "de una manera que tenga sentido", mientras aún se da la bienvenida a "aquellos que quieren estar aquí".</p><p>“Cuando tienes individuos que eligen involucrarse en comportamientos criminales peligrosos aquí en este país, si no son ciudadanos, no deberíamos darles la bienvenida”, dijo López. “No deberíamos darles refugio”.</p><p>Numerosos estudios han revelado que los inmigrantes sin estatus legal son menos propensos a cometer delitos graves que los ciudadanos nacidos en Estados Unidos.</p><p>Un estudio de 2024 financiado por el Instituto Nacional de Justicia que examina datos del Departamento de Seguridad Pública de Texas reveló que los inmigrantes indocumentados son arrestados a menos de la mitad de la tasa de ciudadanos nacidos en Estados Unidos por delitos violentos y de drogas, y a una cuarta parte de la tasa de ciudadanos nacidos en Estados Unidos por delitos contra la propiedad.</p><p>Y según el <a class="Link" href="https://www.migrationpolicy.org/content/immigrants-and-crime" target="_blank" >Instituto de Políticas Migratorias</a>, los estudios han demostrado que los inmigrantes no aumentan las tasas de criminalidad en las comunidades locales donde se establecen. El instituto cita un estudio de la <a class="Link" href="https://www.umass.edu/preferen/You%20Must%20Read%20This/Lee%20Immigration%20and%20Crime.pdf" target="_blank" >Universidad de Massachusetts</a> que conecta la menor actividad delictiva, incluidos los crímenes violentos, con comunidades que tienen políticas de inmigración inclusivas.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: Judge orders federal government to unfreeze CTA funding for Red Line extension
entries.summary: <p>The U.S. Department of Transportation must dispense more than $3 billion in funding it was withholding from the Chicago Transit Authority for the Red Line extension and other projects, a federal judge in Chicago ruled Tuesday.</p><p>U.S. District Judge Thomas M. Durkin granted the CTA's temporary restraining order against the Department of Transportation, which had frozen the funds since last October while the federal government said it was reviewing the CTA's contracting practices for race- and sex-based discrimination.</p><p>The CTA <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/03/20/cta-red-line-extension-lawsuit" target="_blank" >sued the Department of Transportation last week</a>, arguing that the funding freeze would soon halt the long-planned Red Line extension to 130th Street, as well as almost completed Red Purple Line Modernization program. The CTA had secured a legal obligation for $2 billion in federal Red Line extension funding shortly before former President Joe Biden left office.</p><p>Work on the Red Line extension would halt as soon as Friday if federal funds remain frozen, CTA lawyers said in Monday court filing.</p><p>"[U]nless this Court grants relief prior to this Friday ... hundreds of workers will soon be out of jobs and CTA’s ongoing projects will be jeopardized," the CTA wrote in its filing.</p><p>In its own filling, the government claimed the Northern District of Illinois lacked jurisdiction over the lawsuit, and that it should be moved to the Court of Federal Claims.</p><p>CTA Acting President Nora Leerhsen praised Tuesday's ruling as a major victory for the Red Line extension and residents of the Far South Side.</p><p>"CTA promised the community that it would fight for [the Red Line extension], and this ruling is a massive step toward restoration of funding for this historic project," Leerhsen said in a statement.</p><p>The USDOT and Federal Transit Administration, which was also named as a defendant, did not immediately reply to a request for comment.</p><p>The lawsuit is unrelated to the federal government’s pressure campaign on the CTA to improve transit security. The transportation department last week <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/03/19/rta-nita-board-security-survey-cta-metra-pace" target="_blank" >tempered its threat to cut $50 million</a> grants to the CTA after the authority submitted a revised security plan that included increasing police presence on transit by 75%.</p>
entries.author: David Struett

entries.title: Johnson says he wasn't aware Gatewood filed IG complaint against top mayoral aides before firing him
entries.summary: <p>Mayor Brandon Johnson said Tuesday he was unaware before Garien Gatewood was fired that the deputy mayor for community safety had filed a complaint against top mayoral aides who lowered the boom.</p><p>Gatewood oversaw the community safety plan that Johnson credits with historic drops in violent crime.</p><p>Last week, he was summoned into a meeting with Johnson’s chief of staff Cristina Pacione-Zayas and senior mayoral adviser Jason Lee, and told the Johnson administration was “moving in a different direction.”</p><p>Gatewood said Tuesday he filed a formal complaint with the inspector general’s office in October that is currently under investigation.</p><p>He refused to discuss the nature of those allegations, but sources said the complaint accuses Lee and Pacione-Zayas of improper interference in City Hall's contracting process. The sources also said that Johnson was made aware of those contract interference concerns.</p><p>“There have been multiple instances when you attempt to hold senior leadership accountable, there’s a target put on your back,” Gatewood said.</p><p>On Tuesday, Johnson was asked whether he was aware that Gatewood had filed a complaint against his two closest advisers.</p><p>“That was not brought to my attention. It was not brought to any of our attention,” the mayor said.</p><p>Outgoing Inspector General Deborah Witzburg, who has clashed repeatedly with the Johnson administration over a host of ethics issues, refused to confirm or deny that she was investigating Gatewood’s complaint.</p><p>Pacione-Zayas said neither she nor anyone else in the mayor’s office has “any knowledge of a complaint” and they have “not received communication from the IG that this was something that they would be investigating,”</p><p>Asked if she had improperly interfered in the contracting process by seeking proprietary information from Gatewood, Pacione-Zayas said, “I have no idea what he’s talking about. Zero… I have zero intel on it. That’s why that comment came out of left field.”</p><p>Lee could not be reached for comment.</p><p>Johnson said he has a “moral responsibility” to create safe workspaces and if there are “grievances or areas of misconduct, there should be investigations” to determine to veracity of those complaints.</p><p>The mayor scoffed at the suggestion that there is a “culture of intimidation” that has made city employees afraid to report wrongdoing.</p><p>“Clearly, they’re not [intimidated]. Think about how many times you all have covered this. I don’t know of any other administration where people have felt more comfortable” to report wrongdoing, he said.</p><p>“People can talk about how they feel. Especially if they feel like they’ve been treated wrong. That’s not intimidating to me. People should feel comfortable to do that.”</p><p>Gatewood has challenged the mayor to explain the reasons behind his firing and the ouster of his top deputy. He told the Sun-Times that Johnson and his top aides “shouldn’t be allowed to say they don’t talk about personnel matters.”</p><p>On Tuesday, Johnson struggled to explain the policy differences that prompted him to part company with Gatewood.</p><p>He would only say that his singular focus was driving down violence by addressing the root causes of crime, and that there is “more work to do” through youth employment, violence intervention and by investing in long-neglected neighborhoods with entrenched poverty.</p><p>“We lifted up the work as a whole. I don’t believe we’ve ever just simply singled out one individual as responsible” for the progress, the mayor said.</p><p>“This is about continuing the work… but also doubling down on those efforts. There’s more work to be done. I’ll be the first to admit that we still have a little ways to go.”</p><p>Pacione-Zayas was more specific. She said the plan was to “double-down on some efforts” already in place, but “go even more precise and narrow” on those efforts, particularly when it comes to “community violence intervention.”</p><p>“We’ve got to ensure that the money that was appropriated through the budget gets out the door as soon as possible,” she said.</p><p>“Then, we’ve got to obviously ensure that we’ve got the mechanisms in place to be able to track that it is being effectively used and implemented. Victim services — we will continue to provide those efforts, but also track and monitor the outcomes so that we can continue to build the case why it’s important that we provide a comprehensive and sustainable solution well beyond the incident.”</p><p>Lee told the Sun-Times last week that Gatewood cannot claim credit for Chicago's success in reducing violent crime.</p><p>"If we thought that this one individual was the key or the main driver, or that there would be any impact negatively, clearly no decision like this would have been made," Lee said. "Thousands of people outside of government working in their communities on all kinds of things" produced those results.</p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Race to WNBA season starts as owners ratify 7-year collective-bargaining agreement
entries.summary: <p>NEW YORK — The WNBA Board of Governors unanimously ratified the terms of a new collective-bargaining agreement on Tuesday.</p><p>Their vote came a day after the players also unanimously approved the seven-year CBA, which will begin this season and run through 2032. It represents a landmark labor deal for the WNBA and its players.</p><p>"This marks the beginning of a bold new era of the WNBA — one made possible by the passion and dedication of the players, team owners, fans, investors, partners and the entire WNBA family," WNBA commissioner Cathy Engelbert said. "We remain focused on building on the unprecedented momentum around the league and preparing for our milestone 30th season, tipping off in May."</p><p>Lawyers on both sides are writing up the long form of the new agreement, which should be done soon.</p><p>Now there will be a sprint to the start of the regular season on May 8.</p><p>First up is an expansion draft for the two new teams — Toronto and Portland. Rules regarding whom the current teams will be able to protect and how the draft will work are still being figured out. The draft is expected to take place right around the Final Four.</p><p>Next up would be free agency. More than 80% of the league are free agents this year as players had signed deals that were going to expire last year. There are only two veteran players that aren't under rookie contracts who are signed for this season.</p><p>The college draft is scheduled for April 13 in New York.</p><p>Teams are expected to start training camp on April 19 and will have little time to get prepared for the regular season.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" entries.title: Man, 67, gunned down near United Center on Near West Side
entries.summary: <p>Two gunmen walked up to a 67-year-old man and fatally shot him Tuesday afternoon near United Center. </p><p>The 67-year-old was outside in the 2100 block of West Madison Street when two armed assailants on foot pulled guns and opened fire, police said. </p><p>The 67-year-old was hit in the head and taken to Stroger Hospital, where he was pronounced dead, police said. </p><p>Area 3 detectives are investigating. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-400000" entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: United Center owners' massive 1901 Project could get nearly $55 million property tax break
entries.summary: <p>Mayor Brandon Johnson has proposed a nearly $55 million property tax break for the United Center’s 1901 Project — a benefit the arena's owners say is an essential piece to get their self-funded $7 billion project underway.</p><p>Johnson introduced the estimated $54.7 million in property tax incentives to the City Council on March 18. Under Cook County's Class 7b special assessment, the project's property tax rate for the first phase would be 10% for the first 10 years, 15% for Year 11, then 20% for Year 12.</p><p>The 1901 Project’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2025/01/16/first-phase-united-center-7b-redevelopment-approved-plan-commission" >first phase</a> is valued at $500 million. Projects must be valued at $2 million or more to be eligible for Class 7b incentives, according to the city.</p><p>If approved, the tax break would be the first pool of public money boosting the project, which will turn <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2024/02/22/jerry-reinsdorf-united-center-parking-lots-purchasing-white-sox-south-loop-ballpark" >acres of parking lots</a> around the United Center into a vibrant, multi-acre entertainment district.</p><p>There have also been discussions between the United Center’s owners — the Wirtz and Reinsdorf families — and the city on public funding for a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2024/09/27/1901-project-united-center-mega-development-walter-burnett-pink-line-station" >new Pink Line station</a> near the United Center. But that proposal hasn’t advanced yet.</p><p>The property tax incentive has the support of Ald. Walter “Red” Burnett (27th).</p><p>The local alderman said the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2024/07/23/united-center-reinsdorf-wirtz-1901-project-housing-entertainment-hall-retail-campus-arena" >1901 Project</a> will be the largest investment on the Near West Side since the United Center was built more than 30 years ago, ending the area’s long drought in private investment. He anticipates the project will provide millions of dollars in new development — and generate new tax revenue for the city.</p><p>“The 1901 project is a true catalyst for growth on the Near West Side and beyond,” Burnett said in a statement. “I firmly believe we should support those who are willing to step up, invest, and inspire positive change in communities that have too often been overlooked.”</p><p>United Center ownership pitched the 1901 Project in July 2024. It's envisioned as an integration into the surrounding neighborhood, with acres of green space, public parks, athletic courts and a public plaza that will be open year-round.</p><p>Phase 1, anchored by a 6,000-seat music hall, will include a public plaza, boutique hotel and parking deck with a rooftop park.</p><p>A spokesperson for the United Center said Monday that after conversations with city officials, “there is a shared understanding that the project’s public benefits will deliver significant value for the community.”</p><p>“Cook County incentives such as a Class 7B are standard incentives designed to encourage private investment in underserved areas, and this project is exactly that,” the spokesperson said. “Developments across Cook County routinely pursue these types of incentives, and we’ve done so with the understanding that the development will generate significantly increased property tax revenue over time.”</p><p>Phase 1's 800,000 square feet of development will generate $46.3 million more in tax revenue compared to current levels, according to the city. It will also create nearly 2,000 construction jobs, 600 permanent jobs and 180 part-time positions.</p><p>The tax break is the same as <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/casinos-gambling/2025/01/16/ballys-casino-chicago-tax-break-brandon-johnson" >what Bally’s sought last year</a>, though the casino operator was unsuccessful. The 7b tax incentive is for projects that "would not otherwise be economically feasible" in areas that sorely need economic development. </p><p>The city’s Department of Planning and Development reviewed the 1901 project and found it met the requirements for 7b classification, according to the ordinance. </p><p>"DPD reviewed a comparative financial analysis with and without the 7(b), and phase one would not proceed as planned without the incentive due to market conditions, elevated interest rates and private capital return requirements for new and unproven entertainment facilities," a department spokesperson said Tuesday.</p><p>This story has been updated with a comment from the Department of Planning and Development.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2024/07/23/united-center-reinsdorf-wirtz-1901-project-housing-entertainment-hall-retail-campus-arena" >United Center owners planning massive $7 billion campus around the arena</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2025/01/16/first-phase-united-center-7b-redevelopment-approved-plan-commission" >First phase of United Center’s $7B redevelopment approved by Plan Commission</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/casinos-gambling/2025/01/16/ballys-casino-chicago-tax-break-brandon-johnson" >Bally’s rolls the dice on lucrative tax break, without Mayor Johnson’s support</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Abby Miller

entries.title: Donald Trump saddles up a Chicago hobbyhorse — Christopher Columbus
entries.summary: <p>Certain issues just seem to belong to Chicago. Dibs, for instance. That peculiar tradition of trying to claim a parking space with a few scattered chairs, or dinette tables, or whatever, just because you took the trouble of shoveling it out, a weird blend of private effort and public display, these fragile monuments to selfishness. "I went to the trouble to clear the public way, so the space now belongs to me." There's something tragic about dibs; I sometimes snowblow the sidewalks of my entire block; I don't then try to stop people from walking there.</p><p>So it stood out, from the general wrongness of everything happening in Washington, D.C., to see Donald Trump's White House leap into the Christopher Columbus fight, erecting a statue of the great explorer near the grounds of the White House early Sunday morning. Truly, it was as if the president had issued an executive order banning ketchup on hot dogs, not to give him any ideas.</p><p>“In this White House, Christopher Columbus is a hero," a spokesman declared.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-920000" name="module-920000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Opinion bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Opinion</h2></div> </div><p>I bet he is. Columbus's heroism has curdled in some quarters, particularly after the 2020 George Floyd protests, when the exact degree that our government values the lives of people who didn't have the good sense to be born white came into sharp relief. Suddenly, deifying Confederate traitors and rampaging colonizers couldn't be shrugged off quite so easily.</p><p>Columbus statues were taken down in other cities across the country, such as Boston, Richmond and Pittsburgh. Baltimore's was broken into pieces and tossed into the Inner Harbor. The statue erected on the White House grounds, in front of the Eisenhower Executive Office Building, was created by an artist working from the shattered Baltimore statue, fished out of the water.</p><p>Still, Columbus seems a uniquely Chicago concern. Maybe because the city had three Columbus statues go missing in 2020. Maybe just from watching close up the day-to-day gyrations of old-school Italian pride groups trying to make the case that once someone is honored in a public space he therefore must be honored forever, no matter how tastes change. The issue resembles school prayer, where specific groups insist their own private devotions become mandatory public ritual.</p><p>Maybe because Columbus, while known around the world, has been a particular fixation in Chicago, where the city's monumental 1893 fair was dubbed "The World's Columbian Exposition," part of the 19th century trend of celebrating Columbus for discovering the continent. An effort picked up enthusiastically by Italian immigrants, who at the time played the despised outsider role now forced upon Venezuelans. Columbus became demonized in the 21st century for his rough handling of the discovered — raping and torturing and murdering them more than many like to see in our public heroes. (Odd that he would go to bat for him.) </p><p>As a historically minded person, I generally don't like to see statues pulled down. It smacks of the Taliban blowing up Buddhas. There is an enormous statue of Stephen Douglas at 35th Street, just west of DuSable Lake Shore Drive. Douglas was not only a slaveholder, but a bad slaveholder. Yet the edifice remains, and rightly so, because it's historic and in a part of the city not otherwise bristling with tourist sites. Besides, it's also Douglas' tomb, and it could be argued that everyone, no matter how vile, gets to slumber in his own grave.</p><p>Statue smashing is the plucking of low-hanging fruit by those frustrated by society's lack of progress. We might not be able to live in a fair or just nation — we're certainly taking the long road. But we can tear down statues.</p><p>I come to the Columbus dispute as a fan of many things Italian: biscotti, pasta, Verdi, Dante — the list goes on. While not a fan of others — fascism, what Italians call <i>bloccato nel passato,</i> or being stuck in the past. The man Italians chose to elevate themselves in American society 125 years ago is now a millstone, pulling them down. </p><p>The past remains outside of our control. Columbus doesn't care about the condition of his statues, just as the suffering of a single Arawak is not alleviated by removing Columbus statues. What we are fighting over is the future. Trump and his followers are trying to hustle us all back to their imagined past, where Black history is Crispus Attucks killed at the Boston Massacre, and brown races generally exist in rightless limbo, picking strawberries and staying out of sight.</p><p>The White House statement regarding Columbus the hero added: "President Trump will ensure he’s honored as such for generations to come.”</p><p>Will he? That seems to imply Trump will be running the show for generations to come. That certainly seems the plan.</p>
entries.author: Neil Steinberg

entries.title: Despliegan a agentes de ICE en O'Hare mientras continúa el cierre parcial del gobierno que afecta a viajeros
entries.summary: <p>Los agentes del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) estuvieron presentes en el Aeropuerto O'Hare el lunes, luego de que la administración de Trump anunciara que los agentes ayudarían en la escasez de personal de seguridad del aeropuerto en todo el país en medio de la actual paralización parcial del gobierno.</p><p>Al menos una docena de agentes con ropa de ICE fueron vistos en las Terminales 1, 2 y 3 a finales de la mañana del lunes.</p><p>El presidente Donald Trump dejó claro el domingo que <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/22/union-tsa-workers-push-back-demand-pay-after-trump-admin-deploys-ice-agents-in-airports-monday" target="_blank" >desplegaría agentes de ICE en los aeropuertos</a> de todo el país para asistir en las operaciones de seguridad de la Administración de Seguridad en el Transporte (TSA). Trump culpó a los problemas de personal de la TSA, que han provocado largas filas de seguridad en ocasiones, a los demócratas por no llegar a un acuerdo sobre un proyecto de ley para financiar el Departamento de Seguridad Nacional (DHS).</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-e60000" name="module-e60000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>O'Hare fue uno de los 14 aeropuertos donde se preveía el despliegue de agentes de ICE, según CNN.</p><p>Se esperaba que cerca de 75 agentes de ICE estuvieran asignados en O'Hare, según fuentes familiarizadas con el plan. Estaban monitoreando los carriles de salida y puntos de control, no realizando inspecciones de seguridad.</p><p>Los agentes vistos en O'Hare el lunes por un reportero del Sun-Times llevaban en su mayoría mascarillas, en contra de los deseos de Trump, quien dijo que prefiere que no las usen.</p><p>"Soy un GRAN defensor de que los agentes de ICE usen mascarillas mientras buscan y se ven obligados a lidiar con criminales peligrosos", escribió Trump el lunes en las redes sociales, y agregó, "Sin embargo, agradecería enormemente que NO USEN MASCARILLAS cuando ayuden a nuestro país a salir del CAOS causado por los demócratas en los aeropuertos, etc.".</p><p>El gobernador JB Pritzker dijo que el despliegue de agentes de ICE en los aeropuertos está "causando caos e infundiendo miedo".</p><p>“Es hora de que los aeropuertos de nuestra nación vuelvan a la normalidad”, comentó Pritzker en una publicación en redes sociales.</p><p>El alcalde Brandon Johnson declaró el lunes en un comunicado que su oficina está utilizando "todas las herramientas que tenemos para asegurar que las personas, sin importar su estatus migratorio, puedan viajar a y de Chicago de manera segura y sin acoso por parte del gobierno federal".</p><p>El representante Jesús "Chuy" García dijo que la presencia de agentes de ICE en los aeropuertos "no está haciendo que los viajeros estén más seguros ni abordando las largas filas de seguridad".</p><p>"En cambio, esta acción sin precedentes está haciendo que la gente tenga miedo de ir al aeropuerto y causando confusión y violencia, como el incidente anoche en San Francisco", dijo García, refiriéndose a la detención de una mujer y su hija por parte de agentes de ICE el domingo en el Aeropuerto de San Francisco.</p><p>Algunos viajeros de O'Hare dicen que ICE no debería estar en el aeropuerto</p><p>Luke Hoover y Mary Dickey, de North Center, se prepararon para largas filas de seguridad y llegaron tres horas antes de su vuelo a Nueva Zelanda para su luna de miel.</p><p>La pareja, al igual que varias personas que hablaron con el Sun-Times y WBEZ el lunes, dijo que la presencia de agentes de ICE les hizo dudar.</p><p>"No somos grandes fanáticos de esto”, dijo Hoover.</p><p>"Sólo [el] proceso de la TSA, tener personas que no están capacitadas en el proceso tratando de... agilizar el proceso, simplemente no tiene sentido para mí", dijo Dickey.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-0e34f0" name="image-0e34f0"></a> <picture data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Un agente de ICE revisa su teléfono mientras está de guardia en el punto de salida del área de llegadas en la Terminal 1 del Aeropuerto O’Hare el lunes.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Abdul Qahar Karimi, de 27 años, dijo que vio a dos agentes de ICE armados cuando se bajó de su vuelo a Chicago desde Omaha, Nebraska. Karimi, un conductor de camión que estaba en la ciudad por trabajo, dijo que tiene amigos con tarjeta de residencia que fueron detenidos en Omaha.</p><p>Aunque los agentes no hicieron que Karimi se sintiera menos seguro, dijo que aún prefiere que estén alejados de los aeropuertos.</p><p>"No deberían estar aquí porque el aeropuerto tiene su propia seguridad, su propia policía", destacó Karimi.</p><p>Trump menciona posible despliegue de la Guardia Nacional en aeropuertos</p><p>Trump dijo el lunes que consideraría llamar a la Guardia Nacional para ayudar con la seguridad en los aeropuertos.</p><p>“Quiero agradecer a ICE porque intervinieron de manera muy, muy contundente. Harán un gran trabajo. Y si eso no es suficiente, traeré a la Guardia Nacional”, dijo Trump.</p><p>La Guardia Nacional no respondió de inmediato a una petición de comentarios.</p><p>Mientras tanto, el “zar de la frontera” de la Casa Blanca, Tom Homan, dijo el lunes que esperaba que los agentes fueran recibidos con protestas.</p><p>“Primero [los] vamos a enviar a los aeropuertos más grandes con las mayores filas de espera”, comentó Homan en el programa de Chris Cuomo en SiriusXM.</p><p>Cuando se le preguntó si eso significaba que ya se estaba preparando para protestas, Homan respondió, “Estoy seguro de que las habrá. Ya saben, en las ciudades más grandes donde ha habido problemas en el pasado, espero que haya protestas afuera del aeropuerto".</p><p>El Sun-Times no vio a ningún manifestante en O'Hare el lunes.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-9e35de" name="image-9e35de"></a> <picture data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/64bb613/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff7%2F21%2Fbd89aeb64a1bb3eac646785b1549%2Fairportice-260324-03.jpg"
