1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9b605fe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff7%2F21%2Fbd89aeb64a1bb3eac646785b1549%2Fairportice-260324-03.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/64bb613/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff7%2F21%2Fbd89aeb64a1bb3eac646785b1549%2Fairportice-260324-03.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Los viajeros esperan el lunes en una larga fila de seguridad en la Terminal 1 del Aeropuerto O’Hare.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>No se vieron agentes en Midway</p><p>No se vieron agentes de ICE en Midway, pero algunos viajeros aún se sintieron afectados.</p><p>Anayeli Castro estaba esperando a su esposo en el punto de control de seguridad con sus dos hijos pequeños. Había oído en internet que debían llegar al menos cuatro horas antes. Pero cuando revisó la aplicación de la TSA para ver cuánto tiempo era la fila de seguridad, la aplicación no estaba actualizando el tiempo de espera.</p><p>"Estoy aquí tres horas antes, por suerte las filas no son largas en este momento", dijo Castro. "Normalmente llegamos dos horas antes, pero cuando escuché que tienes que estar aquí de cuatro a cinco horas antes, me asusté".</p><p>Castro dijo que le preocupaba que los agentes de ICE estuvieran en los aeropuertos.</p><p>"Son agentes de ICE. Es diferente de la TSA, así que te asusta un poco", comentó Castro. “Soy inmigrante, así que es aterrador”.</p><p>El domingo, los funcionarios del sindicato de la TSA dijeron al Sun-Times que están preocupados acerca de la presencia de agentes de ICE en los aeropuertos. Uno de ellos dijo que hay peligro debido a la falta de capacitación de ICE en seguridad aérea.</p><p>Lori Houzer, siguiendo las instrucciones, llegó a Midway más de tres horas antes de su vuelo a Canadá el lunes. Pero llegó tan temprano que no había personal para registrar su equipaje.</p><p>"Hubiera llegado, como máximo, dos horas antes si estoy registrando maletas, que es más que suficiente, a veces una hora y media", dijo. "Ahora son al menos dos horas más. Y eso si las cosas van bien," agregó.</p><p>Houzer dijo que los agentes de la TSA deberían ser remunerados porque eso los motiva.</p><p>"Es un trabajo de seguridad", dijo. "Me gustaría que la gente lo tomara en serio".</p><p><i>Contribuyeron: Violet Miller, Alex Degman, Kade Heather, Mitchell Armentrout </i></p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Araceli Gómez-Aldana | WBEZ

entries.title: Frank Thomas sues White Sox, Nike and Fanatics over jersey sales
entries.summary: <p>Once scolded to “stay out of White Sox business,” Hall of Famer Frank Thomas now is suing the team for allegedly cutting him out of it.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2024/03/24/frank-thomas-greatness-only-strengthens-over-time-making-him-the-top-player-in-white-sox-team-history" target="_blank" ><u>greatest hitter in Sox history</u></a> argues the team is profiting off his name and likeness on a new line of jerseys without compensation or his consent, according to a lawsuit filed by Thomas last week in Cook County Circuit Court.</p><p>He’s disputing the use of his name and iconic No. 35 on the red-and-black “City Connect 2.0” jerseys that were <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2025/04/28/white-sox-unveil-2025-city-connect-uniforms" target="_blank" ><u>unveiled last spring</u></a> by the team in partnership with Nike and Fanatics Inc., which also are listed as defendants in the suit.</p><p>The team and companies “were unjustly enriched through substantial commercial benefits,” according to Thomas, who never agreed to the use and “has received no compensation or other consideration,” his suit says.</p><p>That “violates fundamental principles of justice, equity, good conscience, and fair play,” according to the suit penned by attorney Thomas Demetrio.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-fd0000" name="image-fd0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e97c16b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1732x972+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F49%2F50%2F7c99b89a4383a085bdc51f5d8e90%2Fbighurtsuit.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9a5890d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1732x972+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F49%2F50%2F7c99b89a4383a085bdc51f5d8e90%2Fbighurtsuit.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/395743e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1732x972+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F49%2F50%2F7c99b89a4383a085bdc51f5d8e90%2Fbighurtsuit.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Images of Frank Thomas jerseys for sale, allegedly without the player's consent. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/395743e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1732x972+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F49%2F50%2F7c99b89a4383a085bdc51f5d8e90%2Fbighurtsuit.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/619fcd4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1732x972+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F49%2F50%2F7c99b89a4383a085bdc51f5d8e90%2Fbighurtsuit.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/395743e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1732x972+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F49%2F50%2F7c99b89a4383a085bdc51f5d8e90%2Fbighurtsuit.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Images included in the lawsuit of Frank Thomas jerseys for sale, allegedly without the player’s consent. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>They accuse the Sox, Nike and Fanatics of violating Illinois’ Right of Publicity Act, which recognizes “the right to control and to choose whether and how to use an individual's identity for commercial purposes.”</p><p>The two-time American League MVP is seeking more than $50,000, plus legal fees and punitive damages.</p><p>Sox spokesman Scott Reifert noted the team doesn’t comment on active litigation. A Nike spokesperson declined to comment. Fanatics didn’t respond to a request for comment.</p><p>The jerseys with his name and others remain on sale for $175 each. The Bulls-style crossover alternates were a quick hit with fans last season.</p><p>It’s just the latest sore spot between the “Big Hurt” and the team whose record book he tops in home runs (448), on-base percentage (.427), slugging percentage (.568), doubles (447) and runs scored (1,327).</p><p>Thomas helped the Sox sell hundreds of thousands of jerseys, merchandise and tickets over his 16 years on the South Side, which ended in a war of words between the slugger and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2024/07/03/former-white-sox-vp-ken-williams-wants-another-title-open-to-returning-to-baseball" target="_blank" ><u>ex-general manager Ken Williams in 2006</u></a>.</p><p>“He better stay out of White Sox business,” Williams famously said of a “whining” Thomas, who also sued the Sox that year, alleging team doctors misdiagnosed a fractured bone in his foot as a bruise and cleared him to play. They settled the medical-negligence suit in 2011.</p><p>Fences were mended enough for Thomas to appear at the 10th-anniversary celebration for the ‘05 champs, but he wasn’t at the 20-year reunion.</p><p>The rocky relationship hit another bump in February when a Black History Month post from the Sox’ social media team <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/02/01/white-sox-legend-frank-thomas-upset-with-being-left-off-black-history-month-graphic" target="_blank" ><u>left him off a timeline</u></a> of trailblazing moments for Black players across team history.</p><p>“I guess the black player who made you rich over there and holds all your records is forgettable!” Thomas replied. “Don’t worry I’m taking Receipts!”</p><p>Thomas, who last appeared at Rate Field to throw out a ceremonial first pitch in 2024, was honored in subsequent Sox posts that month.</p><p>A hearing on his suit is scheduled for May.<br></p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: O'Neill Burke rejects bid for special prosecutor to target ICE, says it threatens to 'thwart' justice
entries.summary: <p>Cook County's top prosecutor argued Tuesday that a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-cook-county-oneill-burke-trump-ice-border-patrol-midway-blitz-special-prosecutor" target="_blank" >bid to name a special prosecutor</a> to investigate the federal agents behind last fall’s Operation Midway Blitz amounts to a “frivolous”<b> </b>and “heavy handed”<b> </b>attempt to usurp her authority — and could come at a cost.</p><p>State’s Attorney Eileen<b> </b>O’Neill Burke<b> </b>denied that any conflict exists to warrant the move. She said she’s not intimidated by “vague”<b> </b>threats of political retaliation. And she said a broad coalition’s argument to the contrary actually threatens to “thwart”<b> </b>justice.</p><p>“Acting outside the bounds of the law comes with a price,” O’Neill Burke argued. “And that price here would be compromising the prosecution’s ability to obtain justice for victims and the community.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-cook-county-oneill-burke-trump-ice-border-patrol-midway-blitz-special-prosecutor" >Seeking special prosecutor to target ICE, coalition says O’Neill Burke turned ‘blind eye’ to feds’ violence</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>The commentary came in a 24-page response to the high-profile bid<b> </b>for a special prosecutor, launched earlier this month<b> </b>by a coalition of more than 200 elected officials, clergy, journalists and attorneys. They say O’Neill Burke has turned a “blind eye”<b> </b>to myriad crimes committed by federal agents during the Trump administration’s aggressive deportation blitz last fall.</p><p>The alleged crimes include murder, assault, perjury and even conspiracy. The coalition’s March 12 petition<b> </b>seeking a special prosecutor prominently cited the September killing of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/09/15/silverio-villegas-gonzalez-fatally-shot-by-ice-agents-touched-the-lives-of-so-many-loved-ones-say" target="_blank" >Silverio<b> </b>Villegas<b> </b>Gonzalez</a><b> </b>and the October<b> </b>shooting of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/03/marimar-martinez-shot-border-patrol-chicago-speaks-out-after-good-pretti-deaths-i-am-their-voice" target="_blank" >Marimar Martinez</a>, both at the hands of federal agents.</p><p>Now O’Neill Burke has submitted the most substantive response to her critics to date. She insisted any investigation must be conducted by law enforcement, but also noted that any prosecutor “should be loath to provide information on any ongoing investigation.”</p><p>“Petitioners are claiming that the state’s attorney has an actual conflict because she is not abdicating her prosecutorial discretion to elected officials and the public or succumbing to political pressure,” she argued in her filing.</p><p>O’Neill Burke again pointed to a 2017 <a class="Link" href="https://www.illinoiscourts.gov/resources/bb31d7b0-e90c-4843-8486-c740ae2624ef/file" target="_blank" >Illinois Supreme Court decision</a> she says limits her ability to investigate the agents. But coalition attorney Steve Art insisted that every special prosecutor “takes on the role of an elected state’s attorney and investigates and prosecutes” a crime.</p><p>Art said O’Neill Burke’s argument is “exactly”<b> </b>why a special prosecutor is needed. The coalition he represents has been “asking for justice for months,”<b> </b>he said.</p><p>“Instead of getting any commitment to do justice — to investigate, to prosecute — all we get from the state’s attorney is legal obstacles,” Art told reporters Tuesday.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/public-safety/2026/02/06/how-can-chicagos-top-prosecutor-hold-ice-accountable" >How can Chicago’s top prosecutor hold ICE accountable?</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>O’Neill Burke’s deputies filed her response to the petition during a brief hearing Tuesday before Cook County Circuit Judge Erica<b> </b>Reddick, the presiding judge of the criminal division. Lawyers are due back April 10<b> </b>in Reddick’s courtroom<b> </b>at the Leighton Criminal Courthouse.</p><p>The coalition includes the Chicago News Guild, which represents journalists at the Chicago Sun-Times.</p><p>Prosecuting federal agents in state court would require <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/01/08/can-state-officials-prosecute-the-feds-calls-grow-after-minneapolis-shooting" target="_blank" >showing they acted unreasonably</a> while fulfilling their duties. Meanwhile, prosecutors like O’Neill Burke are entrusted with discretion to choose which cases to pursue. The coalition argued O’Neill Burke’s impartiality “has been irrevocably tainted” by her failure to respond to public demands<b> </b>for an investigation, though.</p><p>In her filing Tuesday, O’Neill Burke repeatedly cited the Illinois Supreme Court ruling addressing the power of a state’s attorney to investigate alleged crimes.</p><p>The court found the duty is “limited to circumstances where other law enforcement agencies inadequately deal with such investigation … or where a law enforcement agency asks the state’s attorney for assistance.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/19/chicago-ice-crime-border-patrol-trump-midway-blitz-alex-pretti-renee-good-eileen-oneill-burke" >Cook County’s top prosecutor outlines plan to charge ICE agents with crimes</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>“Petitioners make no allegation that the state’s attorney has been presented with any credible evidence from law enforcement which she refused to charge,” O’Neill Burke wrote Tuesday, “but instead fault her with not investigating criminal conduct, where they have not established it is her duty to investigate.”</p><p>She said the coalition failed to find any “specific personal interest”<b> </b>creating a conflict. She rejected the idea that she’s susceptible to intimidation by President Donald Trump’s Justice Department. And in any event, she said no specific threat of retaliation has been made.</p><p>“It is common for people to threaten the state’s attorney or her assistants,” O’Neill Burke wrote, “and those threats generally do not engender any concern from outside parties or prompt the filing of a petition to appoint a special prosecutor.”</p><p>O’Neill Burke criticized the coalition for “boldly”<b> </b>seeking input in the process of selecting a special prosecutor. She also took a shot at the law firm leading the charge, Loevy and Loevy, for its handling of separate litigation in federal court last fall.</p><p>The firm helped <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/11/06/judge-sara-ellis-operation-midway-blitz-tear-gas-deportation-campaign" target="_blank" >secure an order</a> from U.S. District Judge Sara Ellis restricting the feds’ use of force during Midway Blitz. But later, after the 7th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals signaled the feds<b> </b><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/11/19/federal-appeals-court-halts-ruling-that-curbed-use-of-force-during-immigration-campaign" target="_blank" >were likely to prevail</a> in the end, the firm <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/12/02/lawyers-who-secured-bovino-testimony-body-cam-video-move-to-end-case-that-challenged-midway-blitz" target="_blank" >sought to dismiss the case</a> voluntarily.</p><p>The appeals court formally vacated Ellis’ order<b> </b>earlier this month, citing in part the voluntary dismissal.</p><p>“These times call for serious people,” O’Neill Burke wrote Tuesday. “Not those who cut and run ‘at the first sign of trouble.’”</p><p><i>Neither the reporter nor editors who worked on this story — including some represented by the News Guild — have been involved in the litigation described in this article.</i></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe src="https://docs.google.com/viewer?embedded=true&url=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/70/0f/0d9f739b4ad8909f116f7deb173c/eobresponse.pdf"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: Delivery robot goes viral after shattering West Town bus shelter glass
entries.summary: <p>Video of a delivery robot rolling through the shattered glass of the bus shelter it destroyed in West Town went viral earlier this week.</p><p>The video, which has amassed 3.6 million views on X as of Tuesday afternoon, captures the moments after the robot went through the glass of the shelter near the intersection of North Racine and West Grand avenues.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">chicago. delivery robot drove through the glass of a bus shelter. this country kicks so much ass. <a href="https://t.co/eLbCmgoZqO">pic.twitter.com/eLbCmgoZqO</a></p>— dass (@not_alive_irl) <a href="https://twitter.com/not_alive_irl/status/2035924083293004105?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 23, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>The incident caught the attention of Ald. Walter Red Burnett (27th), who noted his office was in contact with Serve Robotics, the owner of the robot.</p><p>“We are stressing the importance of public safety and consistent monitoring of these robots to prevent harm to people, infrastructure, and property,” Burnett said in a statement. "We have been informed that the company is in talks with the bus shelter provider, and both parties will look to have it repaired as soon as possible. We are glad that no one was hurt."</p><p> Serve Robotics were aware of the incident and worked with the bus shelter provider to quickly clean up the damages. The company was also reviewing what happened in an attempt to make improvements.</p><p>“We have also been in contact with local stakeholders and are committed to addressing any concerns directly,” Serve Robotics said in a statement. “We take this matter very seriously.”</p><p>Two companies — Coco Robotics and Serve Robotics — are <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2026/delivery-robots-chicago-food-coco-robotics" target="_blank" >operating bots on Chicago’s north and west sides</a> under a <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicago.gov/city/en/depts/mayor/press_room/press_releases/2022/september/PersonalDeliveryDevicePDDPilotProgramPasses.html" target="_blank" ><u>pilot program</u></a> City Council passed in 2022. Coco came to town in 2024, and Serve started deploying its fleet last September.</p><p>Coco’s bots are always virtually monitored by a human, while Serve’s largely drive themselves, with humans stepping in when necessary.</p><p>The pilot program has not been without its critics.</p><p>A <a class="Link" href="https://nosidewalkbots.org/" target="_blank" ><u>petition</u></a> to pause the program until after a public hearing has garnered over 3,700 signatures.</p><p>Serve Robotics CEO Ali Kashani told WBEZ that he’s aware of the push back and the company is open to engaging with concerned Chicagoans.</p><p>“People can reach out to us,” Kashani said. “Every email is responded to.”</p><p>In the 27th Ward, Burnett says he’ll continue to take feedback on the program from residents and local businesses.</p><p>“We will consider their performance in complying with safety protocols before making a determination on their presence in the ward following the pilot," Burnett said.</p><p><i>Contributing: Brandon Kondritz</i><br></p>
entries.author: Mohammad Samra

entries.title: What we know and don't know about TSA wait times at O'Hare and Midway airports
entries.summary: <p>The stalemate in Congress over funding the Department of Homeland Security has left airports understaffed, causing unpredictable wait times in security lines that have at times lasted hours.</p><p>Workers for the Transportation Security Administration, which operates under DHS, have gone unpaid for weeks during the partial federal government shutdown.</p><p>Wait times at Chicago’s O’Hare and Midway airports have not been as long as what has been reported at other airports countrywide, but they have occasionally in recent weeks built up to lengthy waits.</p><p>Here’s what we know so far about how the partial government shutdown is impacting air travel in Chicago:</p><h3><b>How long are wait times at O’Hare and Midway?</b></h3><p>The wait times at both airports have been relatively short, with many travelers this week <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/realtalkstruth/status/2036441969870655909" target="_blank" >reporting on social media</a> less than half-hour waits for security.</p><p>Up-to-date wait times have been mostly unclear because the MyTSA app, which usually shows live wait times, is not being managed during the partial government shutdown.</p><p>The Chicago Department of Aviation also does not track wait times for O'Hare and Midway, </p><p>Security lines at O’Hare on March 16, the day after St. Patrick’s Day weekend — which attracts thousands of travelers — were bottlenecked in the morning, but travelers haven’t experienced such extreme gridlocks since then.</p><h3><b>How many TSA employees are calling out of work?</b></h3><p>The number of TSA workers who have called off at O’Hare and Midway airports wasn’t available.</p><p>Nationally, at least 450 TSA workers have quit altogether since the shutdown began Feb. 14 and thousands have been calling off work, according to DHS.</p><p>Almost 11% of TSA workers — more than 3,200 — missed work Monday, DHS said.</p><p>Sunday’s call out rate of 11.75% at airports nationwide was the highest yet of the government shutdown, according to DHS.</p><h3><b>What is ICE doing at airports?</b></h3><p>President Donald Trump’s administration <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/23/ice-agents-ohare-airport-partial-government-shutdown" target="_blank" >deployed U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents</a> to airports across the country, including O’Hare, Monday in an effort to assist the short-staffed airport security personnel.</p><p>About 75 ICE agents were expected to be stationed at O’Hare, according to Mayor Brandon Johnson. Agents were monitoring exit lanes and checkpoints, not performing security screenings. Agents were not deployed to Midway.</p><p>White House border czar Tom Homan said Sunday that ICE agents could potentially cover exits currently monitored by TSA agents, freeing them to work screening lines.</p><p>It’s unclear if any updated guidance was provided to ICE agents, including whether they would be directed to not wear masks while working at airports.</p><p>Agents spotted at O’Hare on Monday by a Sun-Times reporter were mostly wearing face masks, against the wishes of Trump, who said he prefers them to not wear masks.</p><h3><b>Will the National Guard be deployed? </b></h3><p>Trump told reporters Monday that if bringing ICE agents to airports doesn’t help alleviate long wait times, he would consider also deploying the National Guard to help.</p><p>The National Guard did not say whether it has received communication from the Trump administration about potential deployment.</p><h3><b>When will this all be over?</b></h3><p>Senators raced Tuesday to clinch an emerging proposal to end the DHS shutdown by funding much of the department, including the TSA airport workers going without pay, but excluding the ICE enforcement operations that have been core to the dispute.</p><p>While the Trump administration has blamed Democrats over the DHS funding lapse, Democrats have blamed the Trump administration and Republicans for not agreeing to their offer to fund all of the DHS except for ICE. </p><p>"There is one solution that immediately solves the problem at our airports: pay the people who are already trained to protect us — today," Sen. Dick Durbin, D-Ill., said Monday on the Senate floor. </p><p>Next steps in Congress could move quickly, if lawmakers can reach a deal, or sputter out just as fast.</p><h3><b>What does this mean for me?</b></h3><p>Travelers have been urged to arrive at airports early in anticipation of long wait times due to staffing shortages and an influx of spring break travelers.</p><p>The TSA and airlines recommend arriving at least two hours before a domestic flight and three hours before an international flight — even for people who are enrolled in expedited screening programs like TSA PreCheck and CLEAR.</p><p><i>Associated Press contributed.</i></p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: Sheridan Gorman’s killing puts Chicago back at the center of America's bitter fight over immigration
entries.summary: <p>Months after President Donald Trump ended a pummeling deportation blitz in Chicago, the city and its “sanctuary” policies have been thrust back into the national spotlight following the fatal shooting of an 18-year-old college student.</p><p>As loved ones and fellow Loyola University Chicago students mourn freshman Sheridan Gorman, her death has become a rallying cry for politicians demanding stricter immigration enforcement. The alleged gunman, Jose G. Medina, is a Venezuelan immigrant.</p><p>Trump said Gorman’s killing was “devastating” and placed blame on the “open door policy” of his predecessor, Joe Biden. Other Republicans have directed their anger at Gov. JB Pritzker and Mayor Brandon Johnson. </p><p>“JB Pritzker’s soft on crime policies must come to an end,” Darren Bailey, the Republican challenging the governor in the November election, said in a statement Tuesday calling on Trump and Pritzker to work together. “Illinois families deserve accountability.”</p><p>But Pritzker said the “failures” within the immigration system “extend beyond the borders of Illinois.”</p><p>“They’re national failures — a failure to have comprehensive immigration reform, a failure of the president to follow his own edict to go after the worst of the worst,” Pritzker said at a Tuesday news conference in Springfield. “And in my view, we have a lot of work that we need to continue to do.”</p><h3><b>A tragic killing</b></h3><p>Gorman’s family has said they’re “gravely disappointed by the policies and failures that allowed this individual to remain in a position to commit this crime.”</p><p>“When systems fail — whether through release decisions, lack of coordination, or unwillingness to act — the consequences are not abstract. They are real,” the family said in a statement.</p><p>Gorman was walking with friends along the lakefront early Thursday when she spotted a masked gunman hiding in the shadows, prosecutors said. He fired one shot as the group tried to run away, striking Gorman in the back and killing her.</p><p>Medina has been <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/22/ice-detainer-loyola-student-killing-sheridan-gorman" target="_blank" >charged with first-degree murder</a>, attempted murder, aggravated discharge of a firearm, and illegal gun possession in connection to the attack.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a University Chicago student Sheridan Gorman was shot to death.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Medina was previously taken into custody by U.S. Border Patrol in May 2023, released and then arrested again two months later for shoplifting, according to federal officials. He had an active warrant in the theft case at the time of the killing, court records show.</p><p>Mayor Johnson on Tuesday said anyone who commits a crime in Chicago “regardless of their status,” will be held accountable. </p><p>“The proof is in how our Chicago Police Department swiftly interacted and was able to identify, establish the case and submit to the state’s attorney,” he told reporters, referring to the investigation of Gorman’s slaying.</p><p>Johnson said the purpose of the Welcoming City Ordinance and Illinois Trust Act — policies that prohibit local police from cooperating with federal immigration enforcement — are to ensure “undocumented individuals do not feel intimidated when they reach out to law enforcement.”</p><p>“We know that this President has not taken this situation and this challenge in our country seriously,” said Johnson. “Because my question would be: Isn’t it time for the President of the United States of America and congress to actually act and pass comprehensive immigration reform?” </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-1a0000" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0d00d81/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6523x3661+0+345/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F49%2F3e%2Fe19b05d50555e95d227be987be3a%2Fcouncil-062223-25.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/070431f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6523x3661+0+345/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F49%2F3e%2Fe19b05d50555e95d227be987be3a%2Fcouncil-062223-25.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson presides over a Chicago City Council meeting at City Hall on June 21, 2023. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times file photo</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>As of Tuesday morning, neither Pritzker nor Johnson had spoken directly with the Gorman family. </p><p>“When something like this happens — a tragedy where a young person is gunned down — you automatically think about your own children. You just do,” Johnson said. “There’s no words that one could express that could properly console a family that lost their baby. My condolences to the Gorman family. I’m gonna continue to pray for their strength.” </p><p>When the Trump administration launched Operation Midway Blitz last September, officials said the immigration enforcement campaign was targeting “criminal illegal aliens” who’d flocked to Chicago and Illinois because of sanctuary policies. In the two and a half months that followed, scores of masked agents arrested thousands of people — though the majority had no criminal records. </p><p>Agents, primarily led by outgoing U.S. Border Patrol Cmdr. Gregory Bovino, used extreme force throughout the campaign. They frequently deployed tear gas and pepper balls on residential streets. And immigration agents shot two people, seriously wounding Marimar Martinez and killing Silverio Villegas González in separate incidents.</p><p>After Chicago, Trump sent a surge of agents to Minneapolis. The campaign there turned out to be a breaking point for the administration, which struggled to justify the shooting deaths of Renee Good and Alex Pretti.</p><p>Department of Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem was fired, and Bovino was sent back to his normal post in southern California. He is now retiring.</p><h3><b>‘Demand accountability’</b></h3><p>Gorman’s death is quickly becoming as symbolic to Republicans as the murder of Laken Riley, a Georgia nursing student slain in 2024 by Jose Antonio Ibarra, a Venezuelan native who was in the U.S. without legal status.</p><p>The Trump administration often invokes Riley’s name to justify mass deportations. The talking point remains that “open border” policies by the Biden administration led to such tragic deaths.</p><p>But research, including a <a class="Link" href="https://news.northwestern.edu/stories/2024/03/immigrants-are-significantly-less-likely-to-commit-crimes-than-the-us-born" target="_blank" >Northwestern University study</a> from 2024, shows immigrants were consistently less likely to commit crimes than those born in the U.S.</p><p>Riley’s family in March 2024 told <a class="Link" href="https://www.nbcnews.com/news/us-news/laken-riley-dad-today-interview-rcna143742" target="_blank" >NBC News</a> they did not want her name to be used in public debates about immigration.</p><p>Last year, DHS dedicated Operation Midway Blitz to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2025/09/08/operation-midway-blitz-katie-abraham-illinois-dhs-kristi-noem-donald-trump" target="_blank" >Katie Abraham</a>, one of two women killed in an Urbana drunken driving crash in 2025, allegedly by an undocumented Guatemalan man who fled the scene.</p><p>Trump and other Republicans had previously highlighted the case and called Abraham’s family “angel parents,” a term the administration has used to describe relatives of victims killed by people who lack legal status.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2d0000" name="image-2d0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5f6fe23/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+1/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe0%2F9b%2F405d1d64beea625c9a13c572ce1a%2Ftrump-8.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a022d50/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+1/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe0%2F9b%2F405d1d64beea625c9a13c572ce1a%2Ftrump-8.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8059a1a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+1/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe0%2F9b%2F405d1d64beea625c9a13c572ce1a%2Ftrump-8.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="President Donald Trump" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8059a1a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+1/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe0%2F9b%2F405d1d64beea625c9a13c572ce1a%2Ftrump-8.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/75e3d1d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+1/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe0%2F9b%2F405d1d64beea625c9a13c572ce1a%2Ftrump-8.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8059a1a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+1/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe0%2F9b%2F405d1d64beea625c9a13c572ce1a%2Ftrump-8.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>President Donald Trump pauses while speaking in the Roosevelt Room of the White House on Sept. 4, 2019.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>AP Photo/Alex Brandon</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Both of Abraham’s parents attended a June 2024 House Committee on Oversight and Reform hearing centered on immigration policy, which featured <a class="Link" href="https://www.google.com/search?sca_esv=48c2a6a335f0c113&sxsrf=AE3TifOmLJ3fzeoEjRJmKGo3oWmLf-iKZA:1757367879113&q=pritzker+house+oversight+committee+sun-times&tbm=nws&source=lnms&fbs=AIIjpHxU7SXXniUZfeShr2fp4giZrjP_Cx0LI1Ytb_FGcOviEiaSXTsdMUiuBeEuSNqG6MGxAgmTPrUk32Qw7HMCrZRR7g-RRFFbn0y_7nZVO61ZFxzc_5MXkaRlPgxU0-WniyuYuDOCi-L-sFBhbDPDzYg-nr_k5cQ39efZZoapLOXCB7IAOwWxYmWc7xGYvh-02vK2bh6VlXkJe8qFmbtNc88iSuI5WA&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwi3mrLBkcqPAxVUhIkEHR6dLcQQ0pQJegQIGBAB&biw=1236&bih=686&dpr=2" target="_blank" >testimony by Pritzker</a>, Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz and New York Gov. Kathy Hochul.</p><p>Joe Abraham, Katie Abraham’s father, has become a vocal proponent of Trump’s immigration policies, including heaping praise on the Chicago deportation operation, which he said was about creating an immigration system that was “humane and secure.”</p><p>“It would be unjust and unreasonable to separate my family’s loss from the policies that failed us,” Abraham said in an October 2025 Chicago Tribune op-ed.</p><p>“We did everything right. We worked hard, obeyed the law, paid our taxes, and trusted that government would protect us in return. That trust was broken. Our leaders — from the governor’s office on down — have treated immigration as a numbers game, not as a matter of public safety or national security.”</p><p>Joe Abraham this week wrote on social media that Gorman’s death marked “another young life lost to preventable violence in Illinois.”</p><p>“How many more? Leaders will offer words, then erase this story for political expediency,” he wrote. “Demand accountability. Enough is enough.”</p><p>Denise Lorence, Katie Abraham’s mother, has <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagotribune.com/2025/10/21/opinion-katie-abraham-operation-midway-blitz/" target="_blank" >countered his views</a>, writing in October that her daughter would not want to be associated with Trump’s deportation efforts.</p><p>“Katie would not want to be associated with an operation in which kids witness their parents being taken into custody on their way to or from school,” she wrote. “She wouldn’t support scaring kids with the use of military efforts in their neighborhoods or in their apartment.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/24/heres-what-to-know-killing-sheridan-gorman" target="_blank" >What to know about the killing of Sheridan Gorman</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/23/chicago-crime-trump-sheridan-gorman-loyola-murder-shooting-deportation-immigration" target="_blank" >Trump decries Loyola student’s killing while pushing deportations; suspect’s court date postponed</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/22/ice-detainer-loyola-student-killing-sheridan-gorman" target="_blank" >Man charged with murder of Loyola student Sheridan Gorman</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Notre Dame star RB Jeremiyah Love out of Bears' reach, but other prospects intrigue at pro day
entries.summary: <p>SOUTH BEND, Ind. — As Notre Dame star running back Jeremiyah Love progressed toward becoming a top NFL prospect, the way the league valued his position was erratic.</p><p>As he wrapped up his high school career in 2022, no running backs went in the first round, even after Saquon Barkley’s arrival. Then the Falcons took Bijan Robinson in the top 10. Then none again. Last year, Ashton Jeanty went to the Raiders at No. 6.</p><p>Love, a potential top-five pick, hopes he’ll help solidify how much running backs still matter in the modern game. He totaled 2,497 yards and 40 total touchdowns over the last two seasons and was Heisman Trophy runner-up last season.</p><p>“Each draft class has done a great job increasing the value of running backs,” he said Tuesday at Notre Dame’s pro day. “We had Bijan, Jeanty, me, Saquon — a lot of these backs have been putting on for the position. Them doing what they have done has further propelled the importance of having a good running back.”</p><p>No one has to sell <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> coach Ben Johnson on that, but Love is out of his reach. The Bears will pick 25th when the draft begins April 23, then at Nos. 57 and 60 overall in the second round.</p><p>Other Notre Dame prospects are on their radar, though, and the Bears sent multiple scouts Tuesday.</p><p>Running back Jadarian Price, who excelled even while sharing the backfield with Love, is projected to go late in the first round or slip to the second. Offensive guard Billy Schrauth, a three-year starter who can play center, could be a good option in the second round.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/bjm_bGj0LuQ?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Highlights: Jadarian Price shows off versatility in 2025 | Notre Dame on NBC Sports"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>The Bears must weigh a variety of needs. They have holes at left tackle and safety and should be exploring upgrades at several other spots. They traded for former Patriots center Garrett Bradbury after Drew Dalman’s surprising retirement, but he’s entering the final season of his contract and might not be a long-term solution.</p><p>Schrauth said he has practiced at center throughout his time at Notre Dame and that the Bears inquired about it in a meeting with him.</p><p>“I would take a lot of pride in doing it,” Schrauth said. “Most teams ask about it. If you’re an interior offensive lineman, you’ve got to practice it because you never know when your number is going to be called.”</p><p>The Bears could target Schrauth in the second round and develop him behind Bradbury until he’s ready. They’re already set at guard with Joe Thuney and Jonah Jackson.</p><p>Running back is another interesting position for the Bears after finishing sixth in the NFL in rushing last season with D’Andre Swift and Kyle Monangai. Swift is going into the last season of his contract and Monangai showed promise as a rookie, but Johnson and general manager Ryan Poles could pivot if they see a chance to improve that position.</p><p>While all the attention has been on Love, Price averaged 6.1 yards per carry and scored 20 offensive touchdowns over the last two seasons, in addition to three career special teams touchdowns. There’s certainly a gap between the two, but ESPN rated Price the second-best prospect at his position.</p><p>Love said an NFL team would get “everything you’re getting in me” by drafting Price.</p><p>Picking a running back in the first two rounds is much less likely for the Bears than other positions that feel more pressing. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/01/02/bears-rb-dandre-swift-adapted-to-new-scheme-hard-coaching-and-is-delivering-best-season-of-career" >Swift is still just 27 and coming off his best season</a>, and Monangai finished fifth in his draft class in rushing as a rookie. Johnson can work with that.</p><p>But offensive line is a priority, and it almost always requires teams to invest premium resources in order to get it right. The Bears thought they’d solved that a year ago, only to have Dalman leave and left tackle Ozzy Trapilo suffer a major injury. That makes Schrauth worth a look.</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Finalist for CPS CEO courted controversy in past jobs, but defenders praise his bold vision
entries.summary: <p>When Meisha Porter, the former leader of New York City’s school system, withdrew her name from consideration to become the CEO of Chicago Public Schools last week, the district suddenly found itself with just two candidates for the job.</p><p>One of them is Macquline King, who has been serving as CPS’ interim leader since June. The former CPS principal did not make the cut to be a finalist back in the fall. But some board members have recently warmed up to her, citing how she’s handled contentious issues, such as <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/08/14/majority-of-cps-board-demands-a-budget-with-pension-payment-and-loan-rebuffing-proposal-from-cps-leaders" target="_blank" ><u>the budget process</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/02/12/no-recess-lockdowns-less-learning-how-immigration-agents-disrupted-cps-schools-this-fall" target="_blank" ><u>responding to aggressive immigration enforcement</u></a> that impacted schools.</p><p>Given the <a class="Link" href="https://www.chalkbeat.org/chicago/2026/03/20/meisha-porter-withdraws-from-chicago-public-schools-ceo-search/" target="_blank" ><u>dwindling field</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2026/03/13/cps-board-names-3-finalists-to-lead-district-interim-ceo-king-former-nyc-east-baton-rouge-schools-chiefs" target="_blank" ><u>protracted search</u></a>, King’s standing appears to be rising.</p><p>The other is Sito Narcisse, a former leader of Louisiana’s second-largest school district who has been linked to controversies in his previous roles, leading some to question how he made it to be a finalist to lead the third-largest school district in the country with many red flags.</p><p>His work at times has been polarizing. In Baton Rouge, families sued him and the district over a controversial event he promoted. Early in his career in Boston, Narcisse was criticized for making sweeping changes at the school he led with little or no community input, a concern that would follow him.</p><p>Supporters say Narcisse has been a victim of local politics that have stymied his vision, calling him a “transformational leader” who moves fast to drive change and increase positive outcomes for students.</p><p>For his part, Narcisse says that while not everyone has supported all his moves, he’s made decisions with the best interests of students in mind.</p><p>“In every place I have been, outcomes for students and culture for staff have improved,” Narcisse wrote in an email to the Sun-Times/WBEZ.</p><h3>Lawsuit and ties to ousted leader </h3><p>One of the most notable controversies happened when Narcisse was superintendent of Louisiana’s East Baton Rouge Parish Schools, which he led from 2021 to 2024. It was the only time he’s served as the top leader of a school district.</p><p>Families filed a lawsuit against Narcisse and the school board in 2023 <a class="Link" href="https://www.theadvocate.com/baton_rouge/news/education/parents-students-complain-east-baton-rouge-schools-field-trip-was-more-like-church-service/article_97dc38c2-3a0d-11ed-a388-3f7b65892ceb.html" target="_blank" ><u>alleging the district misled parents </u></a>when it promoted a career fair for seniors hosted at a local church that included overt religious messaging.</p><p>The families allege that organizers split high schoolers into groups by gender and had the boys compete in physical contests while girls were “graphically lectured by pastors and other religious figures about virginity, rape, abuse, and suicide and were told to ‘forgive’ their rapists and abusers,” according to the lawsuit.</p><p>Narcisse <a class="Link" href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=93PB0iA_Uiw&t=116s" target="_blank" ><u>appeared on local television</u></a> to promote the “Day of Hope” alongside one of the co-founders of the organizers, 29:11 Academy, whose name is a reference to the Bible, <a class="Link" href="https://www.2911mentor.com/" target="_blank" ><u>according to its website</u></a>. They talked about how the event could help students find careers and make good choices, but didn’t mention any religious components.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.theadvocate.com/baton_rouge/news/education/judge-keeps-baton-rouge-school-board-in-day-of-hope-suit/article_397de226-8b23-11ef-8a4e-432f40734807.html" target="_blank" ><u>Board members have said</u> t</a>hat they were out of the loop because the district allows the superintendent to award contracts worth less than $10,000 without board approval. <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbrz.com/news/day-of-hope-cost-school-system-only-200-less-than-amount-required-for-school-board-approval/" target="_blank" ><u>This contract totaled $9,800.</u></a></p><p>Narcisse has denied the allegations in the lawsuit, which is ongoing, and the case is heading to a possible trial, according to court records.</p><p>In response to questions about the event, Narcisse wrote in an email that the “Day of Hope” was established 10 years before he became superintendent and he was “not aware of any religious components.” He said “the focus was on motivating students to stay in school, explore their futures, and think about next steps.”</p><p>Michael Gaudet, who was on the East Baton Rouge school board at the time, and is still a member, defended Narcisse, saying the situation was “drummed up” by an attorney “who is a publicity hound.” <a class="Link" href="https://www.theadvocate.com/baton_rouge/news/education/participants-defend-controversial-day-of-hope-event-to-school-board-we-all-enjoyed-it/article_e3fd0e5a-45b8-11ed-bc0d-c7a6b674f638.html" target="_blank" ><u>Some participants said they enjoyed the event.</u></a></p><p>“We could have probably done a better job of monitoring that, but none of the allegations have ever been proven true,” Gaudet said.</p><p>He pointed to the school board’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.theadvocate.com/baton_rouge/news/education/no-evidence-for-day-of-hope-controversy-school-leader-says-board-could-seek-investigation/article_c20ac41a-45b6-11ed-9962-4f58e489f0d1.html" target="_blank" ><u>internal investigation</u></a> which “found no evidential proof” of the claims, though several students and parents spoke to local news outlets to express concerns. Some board members introduced a resolution calling for an independent investigation, but <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbrz.com/news/ebr-schools-superintendent-says-criticism-of-day-of-hope-has-been-unfair/" target="_blank" ><u>that was voted down</u></a>.</p><p>Gaudet said the situation was like “a tempest in a teapot… There was nothing to it.”</p><p>In 2023 the school board <a class="Link" href="https://www.theadvocate.com/baton_rouge/news/education/narcisse-rejected-next-baton-rouge-schools-leader-unclear/article_afc3b158-9539-11ee-84e6-bb9fa648edc7.html" target="_blank" ><u>narrowly voted not to renew Narcisse’s contract</u></a> amid a debate about a pay raise. He agreed to a voluntary buyout and stepped down after three years in charge. <a class="Link" href="https://www.theadvocate.com/baton_rouge/news/education/narcisse-supporters-rally-to-keep-baton-rouge-schools-chief/article_d7191668-9dee-11ee-bc28-0b3178838957.html" target="_blank" ><u>A group of supporters rallied to keep him. </u></a></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatLeft> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a00000" name="image-a00000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Michael Gaudet, a school board member in East Baton Rouge Parish, says his school district parted ways with Narcisse for reasons unrelated to performance.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>East Baton Rouge Parish School Board</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Gaudet said the decision not to renew Narcisse’s contract wasn’t due to his performance. He said that six new board members were voted onto the nine-member body during Narcisse’s tenure, and most wanted to move in a different direction.</p><p>A few years earlier, Narcisse faced a turbulent tenure as the chief of schools at Metro Nashville Public Schools, where he was second-in-command to director of schools Shawn Joseph. Joseph hired Narcisse after the two worked together at Prince George’s County Public Schools in suburban Maryland.</p><p>Joseph’s three-year tenure in Nashville was <a class="Link" href="https://www.newschannel5.com/news/newschannel-5-investigates/what-you-need-to-know-about-shawn-josephs-controversies" target="_blank" ><u>marked by a string of controversies</u></a>, including claims that he mishandled allegations of sexual harassment against a principal and complaints that he attempted to award no-bid contracts. A veteran administrator also accused the district of pay discrimination between men and women in high-level positions and said Narcisse, her boss at the time, <a class="Link" href="https://www.newschannel5.com/news/newschannel-5-investigates/metro-schools/administrator-accuses-mnps-of-pay-discrimination-retaliation" target="_blank" ><u>questioned her performance</u></a> when she dug into the district’s salary data.</p><p>In response to questions from the Sun-Times/WBEZ, Narcisse said he did not make salary decisions when he worked in Nashville.</p><p>The board eventually <a class="Link" href="https://wpln.org/post/shawn-josephs-contract-is-terminated-during-heated-school-board-meeting/" target="_blank" ><u>decided to buy out Joseph’s contract.</u></a> Narcisse voluntarily left the district shortly after.</p><p>Amy Frogge, who was on the school board in Nashville at the time and was among Joseph’s most outspoken critics, said that while Joseph was the face of the controversies, Narcisse likely knew what the superintendent was up to as his No. 2.</p><p>"They were extremely close, they were friends,” Frogge said. "It was a painful few years in Nashville when they were there.”</p><h3>Some bristle at leadership style, while others celebrate it</h3><p>Some teachers and former school board members who worked alongside Narcisse have criticized his leadership style, saying he has a bold vision for education but has sometimes overlooked community concerns.</p><p>Dawn Chanet Collins, who served on the East Baton Rouge Parish School Board when Narcisse was superintendent, said his ideas sometimes clashed with those of longtime staff and community members who felt he needed to better understand the makeup of the district before making changes.</p><p>“Each instance where he made decisions that resulted in public chaos he was advised otherwise by staff who would explain the possible consequences of various decisions,” said Collins, who was often vocal when she disagreed with his decisions. “He appeared to not either hear or not care about the potential issues that were being raised.”</p><p>For example, when Narcisse wanted to equitably deal with staffing shortages ahead of the 2022-2023 school year, he <a class="Link" href="https://www.theadvocate.com/baton_rouge/news/education/as-parents-teachers-protest-baton-rouge-school-re-staffing-school-board-seeks-explanation/article_a1886f92-d619-11ec-b975-874bd1939115.html" target="_blank" ><u>decided to reassign teachers and other staff</u></a> to schools that were understaffed. Hundreds of employees were told their positions were eliminated and they could apply for the vacant positions at other schools or be reassigned.</p><p>It’s the kind of shakeup that would be hard to pull off without criticism. <a class="Link" href="https://www.theadvocate.com/baton_rouge/news/education/superintendent-faces-protest-loads-of-questions-on-re-staffing-of-baton-rouge-schools/article_7509cdf4-d7a8-11ec-aa1e-ab8f10afd905.html" target="_blank" ><u>Some community members supported the initiative</u></a>, saying those speaking out against it mostly came from schools that already had plenty of resources.</p><p>But employees who were happy with their roles were upset, saying it disrupted school communities and programs and took away principal autonomy. Parents protested at board meetings. Several board members criticized Narcisse for not working with them on the changes. One said his <a class="Link" href="https://www.theadvocate.com/baton_rouge/news/education/superintendent-faces-protest-loads-of-questions-on-re-staffing-of-baton-rouge-schools/article_7509cdf4-d7a8-11ec-aa1e-ab8f10afd905.html" target="_blank" ><u>top-down management was “breeding mistrust.”</u></a></p><p>Narcisse’s supporters say his initiatives focus on what’s best for students and the pushback stems from resistance to altering the status quo.</p><p>“When you talk about change, that can sometimes be uncomfortable but it’s for the better good of students,” said Dadrius Lanus, who served as school board president in East Baton Rouge Parish when Narcisse was superintendent and is still on the board.</p><p>“Every superintendent is going to have some pushback from the community, but the question is, the moves that he was able to make, did it better our district? And I would say absolutely,” Lanus said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatLeft> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ac0000" name="image-ac0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/18a9d83/2147483647/strip/true/crop/513x288+0+96/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fba%2Fb5%2Fb6825e184269afeb8252a4e12e3c%2Flanus-2023-1025x1536-resized.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5a050fd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/513x288+0+96/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fba%2Fb5%2Fb6825e184269afeb8252a4e12e3c%2Flanus-2023-1025x1536-resized.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4a206ab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/513x288+0+96/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fba%2Fb5%2Fb6825e184269afeb8252a4e12e3c%2Flanus-2023-1025x1536-resized.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A Black man wearing a blue suit and tie with a pocket square." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4a206ab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/513x288+0+96/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fba%2Fb5%2Fb6825e184269afeb8252a4e12e3c%2Flanus-2023-1025x1536-resized.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b3b8bea/2147483647/strip/true/crop/513x288+0+96/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fba%2Fb5%2Fb6825e184269afeb8252a4e12e3c%2Flanus-2023-1025x1536-resized.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4a206ab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/513x288+0+96/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fba%2Fb5%2Fb6825e184269afeb8252a4e12e3c%2Flanus-2023-1025x1536-resized.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Dadrius Lanus of the East Baton Rouge Parish School Board credited Narcisse with helping to expand kindergarten access and get more high schoolers college credit or career credentials during his time in the district.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>East Baton Rouge Parish School Board</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Lanus pointed to the <a class="Link" href="https://www.cgcs.org/resources/newsroom/urban-educator/digital-urban-educator-junejuly-2025/class-of-2025-graduate-stories/east-baton-rouge-students-make-history" target="_blank" ><u>“Pathways to Bright Futures” program</u></a> that Narcisse spearheaded as an example. The program helps high schoolers earn college credits or career credentials. Lanus also said Narcisse was responsible for expanding kindergarten access in the district, among other accomplishments.</p><p>But to Ursula Parkerson, a teacher in East Baton Rouge Parish, Narcisse “came in like a wrecking ball” and pushed forward with his plans despite concerns from the community. That dynamic led to low morale among educators, said Parkerson, a member of the East Baton Rouge Parish Association of Educators, a local teachers union.</p><p>“He was wanting to make big changes and not really caring, it felt like to us, about who got railroaded in the process,” Parkerson said.</p><p>Narcisse faced similar criticisms early in his career, too. As the leader of a struggling public high school in Boston, he tried several controversial turnaround strategies including eliminating the grade “D,” establishing single-sex classrooms and getting rid of many staffers. Three-quarters of the school’s teachers and administrators left, <a class="Link" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20141017071730/https://www.bostonglobe.com/metro/2012/06/24/troubled-english-high-school-showing-little-improvement-after-three-tumultuous-years-under-untested-headmaster-sito-narcisse/XNwPoz4RLD02uCdWePvEfK/story.html" target="_blank" ><u>according to The Boston Globe</u></a>, and while test scores ticked up slightly, the dropout rate, failure rate and absentee rate all rose, as well.</p><p>Gaudet pushed back on critics who said Narcisse didn’t take input from others into account, though he conceded that Narcisse might have moved too quickly at times in Baton Rouge.</p><p>“I would say his philosophy towards leadership is always what’s best for the kids and his leadership style is very collaborative,” Gaudet said. “I think maybe he tried to do a little too much too fast, but it was in a desire to move the needle.”</p><p>Lanus said he wished East Baton Rouge Parish had kept Narcisse on.</p><p>“If Chicago Public Schools were to select him, they are literally selecting somebody who can take the district to the next level,” Lanus said. “They’re going to get a transformational leader.”</p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: Chicago takes another baby step toward reparations
entries.summary: <p>Chicago is taking yet another baby step along the road toward providing some form of reparations for descendants of African-American slaves.</p><p>Two years after naming his own chief equity officer and charging Carla Kupe with overseeing the work of a $500,000 reparations task force, Mayor Brandon Johnson is launching a public engagement effort to “gather lived experiences of harm of Black Chicagoans.”</p><p>Information about those “systemic harms” — gathered through panel discussions, town hall meetings, public hearings and a public survey — will be used to produce a reparations study that will serve as a “historic step towards acknowledging, addressing, and repairing generations of harm experienced by Black communities,” city officials said.</p><p>Johnson campaigned on a promise to provide reparations and is under pressure to deliver on that promise — and find a way to pay for it — with less than a year to go before the mayoral election.</p><p>The city has established a May 31 deadline for completing a public survey that can be accessed at Chicago.gov/RepairChicago.</p><p>In a press release about the effort, Johnson said his "Repair Chicago" campaign is about “listening to Black Chicagoans across our city, acknowledging the harms of the past and present, and building a path forward rooted in truth, accountability and opportunity.”</p><p>“Your experience is evidence and we’ve placed it at the center of our work,” Johnson said. “By engaging directly with residents, we are grounding this work in the voices and lived realities of the people it is meant to serve.”</p><p>Kupe, the chief equity officer, said it is impossible to “talk about reparation without centering the lived experiences of Black Chicagoans.”</p><p>Repair Chicago is an “opportunity to listen deeply, learn collectively and ensure that community voice is not symbolic, but foundational to the policies and recommendations” that the city ultimately makes to repair the damage done by slavery.</p><p>In November 2019, Evanston made history by establishing a $10 million reparations fund to make amends to that north suburb's Black population for historic wrongs traced to racial inequities. The money will come from a cannabis sales tax.</p><p>A prime mover in that effort was former Evanston Ald. Robin Rue Simmons, who said at a 2022 Chicago City Council hearing that "more than 100 municipalities" had advanced local reparations ordinances.</p><p>"What city more so than Chicago should be advancing reparations for its crimes and harms against its Black community?" Simmons asked.</p><p>"When I led this work in Evanston, I did it looking at a $46,000 household income gap between Black and white Evanston. I imagine it is more so in Chicago. In Evanston, we had a 13-year life expectancy difference. This data was enough for us to advance reparations. In Chicago, it's 30 years."</p><p>The Chicago City Council has had a reparations subcommittee since 2020. Little progress was made during former Mayor Lori Lightfoot’s single term. </p><p>Now-former Ald. Roderick Sawyer (6th), who chaired the Health and Human Services committee that created the reparations subcommittee, was asked to explain the slow pace after a June 2022 hearing on the issue.</p><p>“The mayor is not as supportive as I would have hoped. A lot of us are willing to go further. But there's been some recalcitrance by an administration that does not think this is the way to go," Sawyer said at the time. </p><p></p><p>An early version of the reparations resolution championed by millionaire businessman Willie Wilson had the teeth of an ordinance and called for a series of commitments from cash-strapped city agencies, including free rides on the CTA, free tuition at City Colleges and a bigger share of city contracts.</p><p>That was scrapped because, as Sawyer put it, "It was over the top." That was followed by a version that called for a 16-member commission charged with holding hearings and developing a plan to "ensure equity, equality and parity for citizens of African descent in Chicago who are mired in poverty."</p><p>It would have remained in place for 20 years to monitor and ensure compliance. That version was also scrapped, in favor of the subcommittee.</p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman
